## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Control System (ICS):** A collective term used to describe different types of control systems and associated instrumentation used for industrial process control. Such systems can range from a few modular controllers for simple local control tasks (e.g., a single temperature controller in a lab oven) to massive, distributed, interconnected systems (e.g., a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition — SCADA — system controlling a nation's power grid).

An ICS architecture is the **hierarchical arrangement of hardware, software, communication networks, and human-machine interfaces** that together monitor, control, and execute industrial processes. The key subsystems are:

1. **Sensors** – Field-level devices that convert physical phenomena into electrical signals.
2. **Actuators** – Devices that convert control signals into physical action.
3. **Controllers** – Logic-bearing compute units (PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers) that close the control loop.
4. **Communication Protocols** – Standards (Modbus, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, DNP3, OPC UA) that allow deterministic exchange of process data.
5. **HMI/SCADA** – Operator-facing visualization and supervisory software.
6. **Historian & Engineering Workstations** – Servers for long-term data archival, trending, and configuration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Nervous System

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** An ICS functions much like the **human nervous system**:
> - **Sensors** = skin and sensory organs (touch, temperature, pain) – they *sense* the environment.
> - **Actuators** = muscles and glands – they *act* on the environment.
> - **Controllers** = the brain and spinal cord – they *decide* what to do based on sensor input.
> - **Communication Protocols** = the nervous system (nerves/synapses) – the *pathways* carrying signals.
> - **HMI/SCADA** = the conscious mind – what the operator *sees* and commands.
> - **Purdue Model Levels** = the layered hierarchy from reflexes (Level 0) to conscious thought (Level 4-5).

Just as a severed nerve paralyzes a limb, **a cyber-attack on a protocol link or controller can blind the system or cause dangerous physical actions**.

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics & Boundaries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Availability target of safety-critical ICS:** Often **99.999% ("five nines") ≈ 5.26 minutes downtime/year**.
> - **Latency budget for closed-loop control:** Typically **1 ms – 100 ms** depending on loop class.
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** for PLCs: typically **> 100,000 hours** (~11.4 years).
> - **Reference standard:** **ISA/IEC 62443** – global standard for ICS cybersecurity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Decomposition of an ICS Loop

The fundamental control loop is **Sense → Decide → Act → Communicate**:

1. **Sense (Measurement Layer):** Sensor/transmitter reads process variable (PV) — e.g., a RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector) outputs 4–20 mA proportional to temperature. The signal is linearized and digitized at the input module of the controller.
2. **Decide (Control Layer):** The controller (PLC/DCS) compares PV to the **Setpoint (SP)**. The error $e(t) = SP - PV$ is fed into a control algorithm (commonly PID).
3. **Act (Final Control Element Layer):** The controller outputs a corrective signal to an actuator (valve, VFD, motor contactor). The actuator modifies the manipulated variable (MV).
4. **Communicate (Network Layer):** All these operations occur over industrial networks with deterministic timing.

### 2.2 The PID Control Law (Essential Foundation)

The discrete-time PID equation used in nearly all DCS/PLC implementations is:

$$u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

Where:
- $u(t)$ = controller output (sent to actuator)
- $e(t)$ = error signal = setpoint − measured variable
- $K_p$ = proportional gain (dimensionless)
- $T_i$ = integral time (seconds) – removes steady-state offset
- $T_d$ = derivative time (seconds) – anticipates future error

The discretized (velocity) form used inside PLC scan cycles:

$$\Delta u_k = K_p \left[ (e_k - e_{k-1}) + \frac{\Delta t}{T_i} e_k + \frac{T_d}{\Delta t}(e_k - 2e_{k-1} + e_{k-2}) \right]$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — ICS Components at a Glance

| Component | Core Function | Common Examples | Cybersecurity Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensor / Transmitter** | Convert physical → electrical signal | RTD, thermocouple, pressure transmitter, flow meter, level switch | Spoofing/false data injection |
| **Actuator / Final Element** | Convert control signal → physical action | Pneumatic valve, VFD, motor starter, solenoid | Unauthorized activation (damage) |
| **PLC** | Discrete/logic control, scan-based | Allen-Bradley ControlLogix, Siemens S7-1500 | Firmware tampering, ladder-logic injection |
| **RTU** | Remote telemetry, often wireless | SEL RTAC, ABB RTU560 | Cellular/wired MITM |
| **DCS Controller** | Continuous process control, distributed | Honeywell Experion, Yokogawa CENTUM | Compromise of recipe/batch data |
| **HMI** | Operator visualization | PanelView, WinCC, iFIX | Screen capture, credential theft |
| **SCADA Server** | Centralized supervisory control | Wonderware, OSIsoft PI, Citect | Master server breach = plant-wide impact |
| **Historian** | Time-series data archive | OSIsoft PI, Wonderware Historian | Long-term data exfiltration |
| **Engineering Workstation** | Programming & configuration | TIA Portal, Studio 5000 | Stuxnet-style logic modification |

### 2.4 Communication Protocol Reference Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **OT (Operational Technology) protocols** are the *nervous system* of any ICS. KTU frequently tests awareness of their characteristics.

| Protocol | Layer | Medium | Determinism | Typical Use | Native Security |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Modbus RTU/TCP** | Application | Serial / TCP/IP | Low | Legacy PLC I/O | **None (cleartext)** |
| **Profinet** | Real-time Ethernet | Ethernet | High (RT/IRT classes) | Siemens PLCs | Optional authentication |
| **EtherNet/IP** | Application (CIP) | Ethernet | Medium | Rockwell Automation | CIP Security optional |
| **DNP3** | Application | Serial / TCP/IP | Medium | Electric utility SCADA | DNP3-SA (optional) |
| **OPC UA** | Application | TCP | Medium-High | Modern IIoT integration | **Built-in (sign+encrypt)** |
| **IEC 60870-5-104** | Application | TCP/IP | Medium | Substation automation | TLS optional |
| **HART** | Hybrid | 4–20 mA + digital | Low | Smart field devices | None |
| **Fieldbus (FF, Profibus)** | Various | Twisted pair | High | Process automation | Limited |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

A robust understanding of ICS architecture is the foundation for:
- **Risk assessment** under **IEC 62443-3-2** (Zone & Conduit modeling).
- **Network segmentation** (Purdue Level enforcement).
- **Anomaly detection** — knowing the protocol's "normal" distinguishes cyber-attacks from faults.
- **Incident response** — identifying which layer (sensor, controller, or supervisory) is compromised.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis: Purdue Model — Systematic Architectural Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Purdue Model (ISA-95 / ANSI/ISA-95)** is *the* canonical reference architecture for manufacturing and control systems. KTU frequently asks for its levels, zones, and security implications. Below is a step-by-step *derivation* of why the levels exist and what each contains.

### 3.1 Step 1 — Why a Hierarchical Model? (The Problem Statement)

Industrial processes must satisfy **two conflicting requirements simultaneously**:
- **Determinism (OT side):** Control loops must complete in milliseconds with extreme reliability.
- **Flexibility (IT side):** Business systems (ERP, email, internet) need open, high-bandwidth connectivity.

A flat architecture cannot satisfy both. Therefore, an **ordered, layered decomposition** is required — the Purdue Model.

### 3.2 Step 2 — Decomposition into Levels 0 to 5

The Purdue Model partitions a plant into **6 levels**, grouped into **3 zones**:

**ZONE 1 — Operations / Process Control (OT side)**

| Level | Name | Function | Example Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L0** | **Process** | The physical process itself | Reactors, pipes, motors, tanks |
| **L1** | **Basic Control** | Sensors, actuators, single-loop controllers sensing/acting on L0 | PLCs, RTUs, IEDs, valves, transmitters |
| **L2** | **Supervisory Control** | HMI, SCADA, alarm management, control engineering | SCADA servers, engineering workstations, alarm panels |

**ZONE 2 — Operations / Site Operations (DMZ boundary)**

| Level | Name | Function | Example Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L3** | **Site Operations** | Plant-wide optimization, batch/recipe management, historian, OEE dashboards | Plant historian, batch servers, MES servers, patch servers |

**ZONE 3 — Enterprise / Business (IT side)**

| Level | Name | Function | Example Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L4** | **Business Planning & Logistics** | ERP, supply chain, scheduling, quality | SAP, Oracle ERP, email |
| **L5** | **Enterprise Network** | Corporate IT, internet, remote access | Internet gateway, corporate email, cloud services |

### 3.3 Step 3 — Formal Security Boundary (Conduit & Zone)

Per **IEC 62443-3-2**, the Purdue Model is enforced via:
- **Zone** = grouping of physical and logical assets sharing common security requirements.
- **Conduit** = logical grouping of communication channels between zones (e.g., L3 ↔ L4 firewall).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key security rule:** Communication **must** traverse a **DMZ** between L3 (OT) and L4 (IT). Direct L1↔L4 traffic is a **violation** of Purdue Model segmentation.

### 3.4 Step 4 — Mapping the Control Loop onto Purdue

A single PID loop maps as follows:

$$\underbrace{PV}_{\text{measured by L1 sensor}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{e(t)=SP-PV}_{\text{computed at L1 controller}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{u(t)}_{\text{output to L1 actuator}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{PV}_{\text{L0 process changes}}$$

The L1 controller then reports up to L2 SCADA via the protocol stack, and L2 reports to L3 historian. The data flow is *strictly bottom-up for telemetry* and *top-down for setpoints / recipes*.

### 3.5 Step 5 — Latency Budget per Level (Engineering Reality)

| Level | Typical Latency Budget | Consequence of Exceeding |
|---|---|---|
| L1 scan | 1–20 ms | Loop instability, oscillation |
| L2 ↔ L1 | 50–250 ms | Operator cannot respond in time |
| L3 ↔ L2 | 100 ms – 1 s | Missed alarms, batch loss |
| L4 ↔ L3 | Seconds – minutes | Acceptable for business data |

This is why **IT-grade encryption (heavy TLS, deep packet inspection)** can be disastrous at L1 but valuable at L3–L4.

---

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram

    PROC->>SENS: Physical Variable (T, P, Flow)
    SENS->>CTRL: 4-20mA / HART / Fieldbus PV

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six major components of an Industrial Control System (ICS) and state the primary function of each.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **Sensors** – Convert physical variables (temperature, pressure, flow) into electrical signals.
2. **Actuators** – Convert control signals into physical action (valve opening, motor speed).
3. **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** – Execute ladder-logic / structured-text control programs for discrete and continuous control.
4. **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit)** – Telemetry and control of geographically remote assets.
5. **HMI (Human-Machine Interface)** – Operator visualization of process state, alarms, and trends.
6. **SCADA Server** – Centralized supervisory monitoring and data acquisition across multiple PLCs/RTUs.
*(Valid 6-point list, each with correct function, fetches full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Compare SCADA and DCS systems across any three parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Parameter | SCADA | DCS |
|---|---|---|
| **Geographic Scope** | Geographically dispersed (pipelines, power grids) | Confined to a single plant/site |
| **Control Type** | Supervisory; relies on remote PLCs/RTUs for local control | Integrated control of loops, batch, and continuous processes within one system |
| **Data Acquisition** | Polling-based, event-driven | Continuous, high-speed, distributed |
| **Architecture** | Master-slave, often WAN-based | Distributed controllers on a plant LAN/bus |
| **Redundancy** | Often single-master with backup | Built-in hot-redundant controllers & I/O |

*(Any 3 valid contrasting points with correct technical content = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake:** Confusing *Purdue Model levels* with *OSI layers* (they are different conceptual models).
> - **Common mistake:** Writing "SCADA is more secure than DCS" — wrong; security depends on configuration, not system class.
> - **Common mistake:** Omitting the **DMZ/IDMZ** boundary between L3 and L4. Always draw and label the **DMZ** explicitly.
> - **Common mistake:** Stating Modbus is encrypted (it is **NOT** — cleartext by default).

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Purdue Reference Model** for industrial control systems. Clearly state the **six levels** and the **three zones**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **cybersecurity implications** of violating the Purdue Model segmentation. Give **two real-world attack examples**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Purdue Reference Model Explanation (7 marks):**

The Purdue Reference Model (ISA-95) is a **6-level hierarchical framework** that segments an industrial enterprise into logical layers separating physical process control (OT) from business operations (IT). It enforces **defense-in-depth** by enforcing **controlled, directional information flow** between levels.

*Diagram (3 marks):* Show the 6-level pyramid with DMZ between L3 and L4.

**Level Description (4 marks — 0.5 each + 1 for zone mapping):**
- **L0 – Process:** Physical plant (tanks, pipes, motors, reactors). Pure physical phenomena.
- **L1 – Basic Control:** Sensors, actuators, PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers — execute the closed-loop control.
- **L2 – Supervisory Control:** HMI, SCADA, alarm management, engineering workstations.
- **L3 – Site Operations:** Plant-wide historian, MES, batch management, OEE dashboards.
- **L4 – Business Planning & Logistics:** ERP, supply chain, scheduling, quality systems.
- **L5 – Enterprise Network:** Corporate IT — internet, email, cloud, remote access.

**Zones:**
- **Zone 1 (Levels 0–2):** Process Control / Operations
- **Zone 2 (Level 3):** Site Operations
- **Zone 3 (Levels 4–5):** Enterprise / Business IT
- **IDMZ** sits between L3 and L4 as the controlled boundary.

---

**(b) Cybersecurity Implications of Violation + Real Examples (7 marks):**

**Implications (4 marks):**
1. **Lateral Movement:** A breach in IT (L4) directly reaches L1 controllers, enabling physical sabotage (e.g., Stuxnet).
2. **Loss of Segmentation:** Flattening Purdue levels means a single malware infection spreads plant-wide.
3. **Exposure to Internet-borne Threats:** Direct L1↔L5 connectivity brings ransomware, phishing, and worm infections into OT.
4. **Loss of Determinism:** IT traffic on OT networks causes latency/jitter, destabilizing control loops.

**Real-World Examples (3 marks — 1.5 each):**
1. **Stuxnet (2010):** USB-borne worm crossed the IT/OT boundary, modified Siemens S7-300 PLC logic at L1, and destroyed Iranian uranium centrifuges by manipulating VFD frequencies.
2. **Triton/TRISIS (2017):** Attackers penetrated the corporate IT (L4) and pivoted to L2/L1, deploying malware on Schneider Triconex safety PLCs, attempting to disable emergency shutdown systems at a Saudi petrochemical plant.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Describe the **four major components** of an ICS: **sensors, actuators, controllers, and communication protocols**. For each, give one industrial example and one associated cybersecurity threat. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **three common industrial network topologies** (Star, Ring, Bus) with one advantage and one disadvantage each. Indicate which Purdue level each is most suitable for. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Major ICS Components (7 marks — 1.75 each):**

1. **Sensors:** Detect physical variables.
   - *Example:* Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD) in a chemical reactor.
   - *Threat:* **False Data Injection** — attacker alters readings, causing controller to drive the process to unsafe conditions.

2. **Actuators:** Execute physical changes.
   - *Example:* Pneumatic control valve in a refinery.
   - *Threat:* **Unauthorized actuation** — forcing a valve fully open/closed leading to overpressure or release.

3. **Controllers:** Run control logic (PID, ladder).
   - *Example:* Allen-Bradley ControlLogix PLC.
   - *Threat:* **Logic tampering** — uploading malicious ladder logic (Stuxnet-style).

4. **Communication Protocols:** Carry data between layers.
   - *Example:* Modbus TCP between SCADA and PLC.
   - *Threat:* **Man-in-the-Middle / Replay attacks** — cleartext protocol allows command injection and spoofing.

---

**(b) Three Industrial Network Topologies (7 marks — ~2.3 each):**

**1. Star Topology:**
- *Description:* All nodes connect to a central switch/hub.
- *Advantage:* Single-node failure does not affect others; easy troubleshooting.
- *Disadvantage:* Central switch is a single point of failure.
- *Suitable Purdue Level:* **L1–L2** in modern DCS networks (e.g., Profinet, EtherNet/IP).

**2. Ring Topology:**
- *Description:* Nodes form a closed loop; data passes token-by-token.
- *Advantage:* No central failure; deterministic latency via token passing.
- *Disadvantage:* One cable/node break disables entire network (mitigated by **dual-ring** redundancy).
- *Suitable Purdue Level:* **L1 fieldbus** (e.g., Token Ring, FDDI, MediaREDUNDANCY in Profinet).

**3. Bus Topology:**
- *Description:* All nodes share a common trunk cable; master-slave polling.
- *Advantage:* Low cabling cost, simple to install, ideal for long-distance telemetry.
- *Disadvantage:* Collision-prone, low bandwidth, difficult troubleshooting.
- *Suitable Purdue Level:* **L1–L0** in legacy systems (e.g., **Modbus RTU** on RS-485, Foundation Fieldbus H1).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — ICS Architecture & Purdue Model**

**Core Components:**
- ☐ **Sensors** = measurement (PV) — examples: RTD, pressure transmitter, flow meter.
- ☐ **Actuators** = physical action — examples: control valve, VFD, solenoid.
- ☐ **Controllers** = decision-making — PLC (discrete), DCS (continuous), RTU (remote).
- ☐ **Protocols** = communication — Modbus, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, DNP3, OPC UA.
- ☐ **HMI/SCADA** = operator interface; **Historian** = data archive.

**Purdue Model — Must Memorize:**
- ☐ **L0** Process → **L1** Basic Control → **L2** Supervisory → **L3** Site Operations → **L4** Business → **L5** Enterprise.
- ☐ **IDMZ** sits strictly between **L3 and L4**.
- ☐ **Zone 1** = L0–L2 (OT); **Zone 2** = L3 (Site); **Zone 3** = L4–L5 (IT).

**Protocol Awareness:**
- ☐ **Modbus** = cleartext, no auth, most attacked protocol.
- ☐ **OPC UA** = modern, built-in encryption & authentication (IEC 62443 friendly).
- ☐ **DNP3** = electric utility SCADA; DNP3-SA adds security.
- ☐ **Profinet IRT** = sub-millisecond determinism for motion control.

**Network Topologies:**
- ☐ **Star** = modern DCS, switch-centric.
- ☐ **Ring** = fieldbus with redundancy.
- ☐ **Bus** = legacy RS-485 Modbus.

**Security Mapping:**
- ☐ **IEC 62443-3-2** = Zone & Conduit methodology.
- ☐ **IEC 62443-3-3** = System Security Requirements (SL1–SL4).
- ☐ **Stuxnet** = crossed IT/OT boundary, modified S7-300 PLCs.
- ☐ **Triton/TRISIS** = attacked safety instrumented systems (SIS).
- ☐ **False Data Injection** = sensor-level attack.

**Key Equations & Formulas:**
- ☐ **PID:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$
- ☐ **Error:** $e(t) = SP - PV$
- ☐ **Five-nines availability** = 99.999% ≈ 5.26 min/yr downtime.

**Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ☐ Don't confuse **Purdue levels** with **OSI layers**.
- ☐ Don't state **Modbus is secure** — it isn't.
- ☐ Don't omit the **DMZ** in Purdue diagrams.
- ☐ Don't claim **all protocols need TLS** — TLS at L1 breaks determinism.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT742 - CYBER SECURITY FOR INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Cyber Threat Landscape** refers to the entire ecosystem of adversarial actors, attack vectors, vulnerabilities, malware families, and socio-technical risks that endanger the confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the **CIA Triad**, extended in ICS to **Safety, Availability, and Integrity** as per **IEC 62443**) of Industrial Control Systems (ICS), SCADA systems, DCS, PLCs, RTUs, and their associated IT/OT networks.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the threat landscape is the **dynamic, continuously evolving catalogue of known, unknown, and emerging cyber threats** that specifically target Operational Technology (OT) environments — distinguished from traditional IT by its zero-tolerance for downtime, life-safety implications, and proprietary industrial protocols (Modbus, DNP3, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, S7Comm, IEC 61850, OPC UA).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Factory Floor Analogy):** Imagine a modern hospital ICU. Now replace the patients with chemical reactors, turbines, or assembly robots. The "heart monitor" is a SCADA HMI. The "IV drip" is a PLC-controlled valve. A cyber threat in ICS is like someone hacking the heart monitor — they don't need to kill the patient (encrypt files) — they can simply send a false "all-vitals-normal" reading while quietly reprogramming the IV pump to deliver a lethal dose. The goal of attackers is no longer just data theft; it is **physical-world manipulation, process disruption, or extortion of physical assets**.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

- Direct mapping to **Course Outcome CO2**: *Analyse the threat landscape and vulnerabilities in industrial automation environments.*
- Module 2 forms the **foundational threat taxonomy** on which Module 3 (Defensive Architecture) and Module 4 (Incident Response) are built.
- Board questions frequently (≥80% probability in ESE) test the **classification of threats** and **recall of at least one historical case study** like *Stuxnet*, *BlackEnergy*, or *TRITON*.

## 1.4 ICS vs IT — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Purdue Model Reality:** Traditional IT security revolves around the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability). In ICS, the priority hierarchy is **inverted**:
> 1. **Human & Environmental Safety** (topmost)
> 2. **Process Availability & Continuity** (24×7×365 uptime)
> 3. **Integrity of Physical Process**
> 4. **Confidentiality** (lowest priority)
>
> A perfectly secure ICS that stops production is a **failed** ICS.

## 1.5 Categorical Boundary of the Topic

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      ICS THREAT LANDSCAPE (Module 2 Scope)                     │
├──────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┤
│   1. Threat Actors       │  Nation-states, APTs, insiders,     │
│                          │  hacktivists, cybercriminals        │
├──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
│   2. Threat Vectors      │  Malware, Ransomware, Phishing,     │
│                          │  Supply-chain, Removable media,     │
│                          │  Wireless, Remote access            │
├──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
│   3. Threat Types        │  Disruption, Destruction, Espionage,│
│                          │  Extortion, Manipulation            │
├──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Layer Threat Model for ICS

Industrial threats must be analysed at five interacting layers. KTU examiners love this taxonomy.

### Layer 1 — Threat Actors (The "Who")

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Threat Actor** is any individual, group, or entity responsible for an event that has — or could have — an impact on the safety, security, or operation of an industrial system. Source: **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1**.

| Actor Type | Motivation | Capability | Typical Targets | ICS Example |
|------------|------------|------------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Nation-State / APT** | Geopolitical, sabotage | **Very High** | Power grids, nuclear, defence | Stuxnet (US/Israel → Iran) |
| **Organised Cybercrime** | Financial extortion | High | Energy, manufacturing, pharma | DarkSide → Colonial Pipeline |
| **Insider (Malicious)** | Revenge, ideology, profit | Variable (high access) | Any critical infra | Maroochy Water (2000) |
| **Insider (Unwitting)** | Negligence, social engineering | Low | Any organisation | USB-borne Stuxnet drop |
| **Hacktivist** | Ideology, publicity | Medium | Utilities, oil & gas | Dragonfly campaign |
| **Terrorist Group** | Mass harm, chaos | Medium | Mass-transit, utilities | (Speculative, low occurrence) |
| **Competitor / Corporate** | IP theft, sabotage | Medium-High | Manufacturing, R&D | Duqu espionage campaign |
| **Script Kiddie / Hobbyist** | Curiosity, fame | Low | Internet-exposed HMIs | Shodan-discovered PLCs |

### Layer 2 — Threat Vectors (The "How")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threat Vector** = the pathway or mechanism used by an actor to deliver an attack.

**Primary ICS Threat Vectors (per ICS-CERT / CISA advisories):**

1. **Spear-Phishing & Social Engineering** — emails targeting engineers with PLC ladder-logic attachments.
2. **Removable Media (USB)** — air-gapped networks are breached via contractor laptops (Stuxnet's original infection path).
3. **Remote Services Exposure** — exposed RDP, VNC, TeamViewer, Telnet on plant HMIs.
4. **Supply Chain Compromise** — malicious code injected at vendor (SolarWinds → ICS downstream).
5. **Wireless / RF** — rogue Wi-Fi, Zigbee exploitation in sensor networks.
6. **Third-Party Connectivity** — vendor remote-maintenance VPNs.
7. **Internet-Exposed ICS Components** — Shodan shows >100,000 exposed HMI/PLCs globally.
8. **Engineering Workstation Compromise** — infected laptops crossing IT/OT boundary.

### Layer 3 — Threat Types / Malware Categories

> [!NOTE]
> **Kaspersky/Dragos ICS-CERT nomenclature:** Threats in ICS are classified by **intended physical outcome**, not just by malware family name.

| Malware Family | Primary Type | ICS-Specific? | Example |
|----------------|-------------|---------------|---------|
| **Stuxnet** | Worm + Rootkit + PLC payload | ✅ Yes (Siemens S7-300) | Reprogrammed centrifuges |
| **Havex / Dragonfly** | RAT + Backdoor | ✅ Yes (OPC scanner) | Energy sector espionage |
| **BlackEnergy 3** | Modular trojan + KillDisk | ✅ Yes (HMI destruction) | Ukraine 2015 blackout |
| **Industroyer / CrashOverride** | ICS protocol-aware malware | ✅ Yes (IEC 60870-5-104) | Kyiv substation 2016 |
| **TRITON / TRISIS** | Safety-system malware (SIS) | ✅ Yes (Schneider Triconex) | Saudi Arabia 2017 |
| **NotPetya** | Wiper disguised as ransomware | ⚠️ Collateral (ICS-victims) | Maersk, Merck, Cadbury |
| **WannaCry** | Ransomware worm | ⚠️ Collateral | Honda, Renault factories |
| **Colonial Pipeline / DarkSide** | Ransomware-as-a-Service | ❌ IT ransomware, OT victim | US fuel disruption 2021 |
| **Industroyer2** | Variant of Industroyer | ✅ Yes | Ukraine 2022 |

### Layer 4 — Insider Threats (The Most Underrated Risk)

> [!WARNING]
> **Insider Threat** (per **CERT Insider Threat Center, CMU-SEI**): *“A current or former employee, contractor, or business partner who has — or had — authorised access to an organisation's network, system, or data and intentionally exceeded or misused that access in a manner that negatively affected the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the organisation's information or information systems.”*

**Three Insider Sub-Categories (Mandatory for KTU):**

1. **Malicious Insider** — Deliberate sabotage. *Example:* Vitek Boden (Maroochy Shire, Australia, 2000) — ex-contractor dumped **800,000 litres of sewage** into rivers by radio commands to sewage SCADA pumps.
2. **Negligent Insider** — Unintentional, poor cyber hygiene. *Example:* Davis-Besse Nuclear Plant (USA, 2003) — Slammer worm entered via a contractor's infected laptop.
3. **Compromised Insider** — External attacker uses insider credentials via phishing or credential dumps.

### Layer 5 — The Cyber Kill Chain Adapted for ICS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Lockheed Martin's **Cyber Kill Chain** (7 stages) when adapted for ICS (per **SANS ICS Kill Chain**) elongates to **two-stage attack**: first the *IT compromise*, then the *OT lateral movement*.

| Stage | Description | ICS-Tailored Action |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1. Reconnaissance | OSINT, Shodan, vendor brochures | Identify PLC brands, ICS vendor |
| 2. Weaponisation | Build malware (custom ICS payload) | Craft PLC ladder-logic payload |
| 3. Delivery | Spear-phish, USB, watering hole | Target engineer, contractor |
| 4. Exploitation | Zero-day, CVE in HMI/RDP | Compromise engineering workstation |
| 5. Installation | RAT, rootkit | Pivot from IT to OT network |
| 6. Command & Control | Beacon to attacker server | Long dwell-time (Stuxnet: months) |
| 7. Actions on Objectives | Manipulate physical process | Reprogram PLC, blind operators |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Framework Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a Cyber-Security module, the "formulas" are replaced by **mandatory analytical frameworks** that examiners mark against. Memorise this table.

| Framework / Concept | Source Authority | One-Line Exam Definition |
|---------------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **CIA Triad** | Classic IT | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability |
| **ICS Priority Order** | IEC 62443-1-1 | **Safety → Availability → Integrity → Confidentiality** |
| **Purdue Model** | ISA-99 / IEC 62443 | Levels 0–5: Process → Control → Operations → Enterprise |
| **Cyber Kill Chain** | Lockheed Martin | 7 stages: Recon → C2 → Actions |
| **ICS Kill Chain** | SANS | Two-stage IT-compromise + OT-lateral movement |
| **STRIDE** | Microsoft | Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info-disclosure, DoS, Elevation |
| **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** | MITRE | 11 Tactic categories, 78 techniques for OT |
| **Diamond Model** | CRITEC/Calas | Adversary, Capability, Infrastructure, Victim |
| **Threat = Actor + Vector + Vulnerability + Impact** | NIST SP 800-30 | Compound risk equation |
| **Insider Threat = Intent + Capability + Opportunity** | CERT/CMU-SEI | Three-factor insider model |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plant Safety Engineers** use the *threat taxonomy* to map asset–threat pairs in HAZOP + Cyber studies (combined **CHAZOP**).
- **SCADA Designers** use the **Purdue Model** to segregate networks.
- **CISO / Risk Officers** quantify ICS risk using frameworks like **NIST CSF** (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) overlaid on the threat landscape.
- **Insurance Underwriters** use case-study loss data (e.g., *NotPetya cost Maersk $300M, Merck $870M*) to price industrial cyber-insurance premiums.

## 2.4 The OT-IT Convergence — Why Threat Landscape Is Exploding

```
   Pre-2000                Post-2010                Post-2020
   ────────                ─────────                ─────────
   Air-gapped              Connected for ERP        IIoT / Cloud
   Proprietary protocols   Convergence begins       5G / Edge AI
   Manual serial config    TCP/IP in plant          AI-driven attacks
   Few nation-state actors Criminal ransomware boom  Ransomware-as-a-Service
                            Insider market grows     Supply-chain targeting
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever an examiner asks "Why is ICS security more critical now?", the structural answer is: *IT/OT convergence expanded the attack surface; industrial protocols designed without security now ride on insecure TCP/IP; and monetised cybercrime (ransomware) discovered that downtime = ransom leverage.*

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Although this is a non-physics module, the KTU 2024 scheme still rewards **structured analytical reasoning**. Below are the formal derivations of the threat-classification and risk-prioritisation frameworks that examiners expect you to show "step-by-step" in a 14-mark answer.

## 3.1 Formal Derivation of the Compound ICS Threat Model

### Step 1 — Define the basic unit: an ICS Asset

Let $A$ denote the set of all industrial assets in a plant:

$$
A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \dots, a_n\}
$$

where each $a_i$ is a tuple representing a single asset:

$$
a_i = (\text{Type}_i,\ \text{Protocol}_i,\ \text{Criticality}_i,\ \text{Exposure}_i)
$$

For example:

$$
a_{\text{HMI-1}} = (\text{HMI},\ \text{Modbus/TCP},\ \text{High},\ \text{Level-3-internal})
$$

$$
a_{\text{PLC-7}} = (\text{PLC},\ \text{S7Comm},\ \text{Critical},\ \text{Level-1-process})
$$

### Step 2 — Define Threat Actors and their Capability

Let $T$ be the set of threat actors. Each actor $t_j$ carries a capability vector:

$$
t_j = (C_{\text{skill}_j},\ C_{\text{resource}_j},\ C_{\text{intent}_j},\ C_{\text{access}_j})
$$

where:
- $C_{\text{skill}}$ — technical capability (0.1 to 1.0)
- $C_{\text{resource}}$ — funding/manpower (0.1 to 1.0)
- $C_{\text{intent}}$ — motivation strength (0.1 to 1.0)
- $C_{\text{access}}$ — initial foothold or insider privilege (0.0 to 1.0)

### Step 3 — Define the Threat Capability Score

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Threat Capability Score** $\Theta_j$ for actor $t_j$ is defined as the weighted geometric mean (chosen over arithmetic mean to penalise a "zero" in any dimension):

$$
\Theta_j = \left( C_{\text{skill}_j}^{\,w_1} \cdot C_{\text{resource}_j}^{\,w_2} \cdot C_{\text{intent}_j}^{\,w_3} \cdot C_{\text{access}_j}^{\,w_4} \right)^{\tfrac{1}{w_1+w_2+w_3+w_4}}
$$

with standard industry weights from NIST SP 800-30:

$$
(w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4) = (0.30,\ 0.25,\ 0.20,\ 0.25)
$$

**Numerical example — Stuxnet actor:**

$$
\Theta_{\text{Stuxnet}} = \left( 1.0^{0.30} \cdot 1.0^{0.25} \cdot 0.95^{0.20} \cdot 0.40^{0.25} \right)^{\tfrac{1}{1.00}}
$$

$$
\Theta_{\text{Stuxnet}} = (1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.95^{0.20} \cdot 0.40^{0.25})
$$

Computing each term:

$$
0.95^{0.20} = e^{0.20 \cdot \ln 0.95} = e^{0.20 \cdot (-0.0513)} = e^{-0.01026} \approx 0.9898
$$

$$
0.40^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \cdot \ln 0.40} = e^{0.25 \cdot (-0.9163)} = e^{-0.2291} \approx 0.7954
$$

$$
\Theta_{\text{Stuxnet}} \approx 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.9898 \cdot 0.7954 \approx 0.7872
$$

**Conclusion:** $\Theta \approx 0.79$ — *Very High* capability (top of the 0.0–1.0 scale).

### Step 4 — Vulnerability of Asset

Each asset $a_i$ has a vulnerability score $V_i \in [0,1]$ derived from CVE count, exposure, and unpatched status:

$$
V_i = 1 - \prod_{k=1}^{m_i} \bigl(1 - \text{sev}_{i,k}\bigr)
$$

where $\text{sev}_{i,k}$ is the normalised severity (CVSS/10) of the $k$-th known CVE on asset $a_i$, and $m_i$ is the total number of known CVEs.

### Step 5 — The Compound Risk Score for an Asset-Threat Pair

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU High-Yield Equation:** The risk posed by threat actor $t_j$ to asset $a_i$ is given by:

$$
R_{i,j} = \Theta_j \cdot V_i \cdot (1 - E_i)
$$

where:
- $\Theta_j$ = threat-actor capability score (from Step 3)
- $V_i$ = asset vulnerability (from Step 4)
- $E_i$ = effectiveness of existing controls on $a_i$, $E_i \in [0,1]$
- $R_{i,j} \in [0,1]$ = residual risk score

### Step 6 — Aggregate Plant-Level Cyber Risk

The total cyber risk to the plant is the weighted sum across all asset-threat pairs:

$$
R_{\text{plant}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_{a_i} \cdot R_{i,j}
$$

where $w_{a_i}$ is the criticality weight of asset $a_i$ (e.g., 0.7 for a reactor PLC, 0.2 for an office printer).

This equation is what feeds the **plant cyber-risk register** required by **IEC 62443-3-3** and **NIST CSF Identify** function.

## 3.2 The Insider Threat Compound Model (CERT/CMU-SEI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners specifically test this formula in 14-mark answers on insider threats.

The probability of an insider incident is:

$$
P_{\text{insider}} = I \cdot C_{\text{access}} \cdot (1 - M)
$$

where:
- $I$ = **Intent** (malice, grievance, financial pressure) $\in [0,1]$
- $C_{\text{access}}$ = **Capability/Access** (privilege level) $\in [0,1]$
- $M$ = **Monitoring effectiveness** (DLP, SIEM, behavioural analytics) $\in [0,1]$

**Worked Numerical Example — Maroochy Shire case (Vitek Boden):**

$$
P_{\text{Boden}} = I \cdot C_{\text{access}} \cdot (1 - M)
$$

Boden: ex-contractor, holding insider knowledge & radio credentials.

$$
I \approx 0.95 \quad (\text{revenge after job rejection})
$$

$$
C_{\text{access}} \approx 0.90 \quad (\text{still-held radio + system knowledge})
$$

$$
M \approx 0.05 \quad (\text{2000 — no behavioural monitoring})
$$

$$
P_{\text{Boden}} = 0.95 \cdot 0.90 \cdot (1 - 0.05) = 0.95 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.95
$$

$$
P_{\text{Boden}} = 0.812
$$

**Conclusion:** $P \approx 0.81$ — extremely high insider-incident probability — which matches the historical fact that the attack *did* occur 46 times over 2 months before detection.

## 3.5 The Strategic Cost-Benefit Equation (Why ICS is a Soft Target)

For an attacker, the expected payoff of attacking plant $P$ is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\text{Payoff}]_P = (P_{\text{success}} \cdot V_{\text{asset}}) - C_{\text{attack}}
$$

For most ransomware gangs:

$$
P_{\text{success}} \approx 0.30 \text{ to } 0.50
$$

$$
V_{\text{asset}} = \text{daily revenue of victim} \times \text{downtime days} + \text{extortion amount}
$$

$$
C_{\text{attack}} = \text{low} \quad (\text{RaaS kits ~ \$1k–\$10k subscription})
$$

Since $V_{\text{asset}}$ is enormous in OT (Colonial Pipeline: $V_{\text{asset}} \approx \$ 3\text{M/day fuel margin}$) and $C_{\text{attack}}$ is low, $\mathbb{E}[\text{Payoff}] \gg 0$, making ICS a **rational economic target**.

This simple cost-benefit inequality is what KTU examiners want when they ask "Why are ransomware groups now targeting critical infrastructure?"

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any THREE categories of threat actors targeting industrial control systems, with one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Nation-State / Advanced Persistent Threat (APT)** — State-sponsored attackers with very high capability, motivated by geopolitical goals. *Example:* The Stuxnet attack (allegedly US/Israel) on Iran's Natanz uranium enrichment facility in 2010 destroyed approximately 1,000 centrifuges by reprogramming Siemens S7-300 PLCs. *(1 mark)*

> 2. **Organised Cybercrime / Ransomware Group** — Financially motivated, uses Ransomware-as-a-Service models. *Example:* The DarkSide ransomware gang attacked Colonial Pipeline (USA, 2021), forcing a 6-day shutdown and a $4.4M ransom payment. *(1 mark)*

> 3. **Malicious Insider** — Disgruntled employee or ex-employee with authorised access. *Example:* Vitek Boden, a former contractor of Maroochy Shire (Australia, 2000), used his radio credentials to release 800,000 litres of raw sewage across 46 attacks. *(1 mark)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is an insider threat in an ICS environment? Differentiate between malicious, negligent, and compromised insiders with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **insider threat** is the risk posed by individuals who have (or had) authorised access to an organisation's ICS network, system, or data and misuse that access to harm the organisation. *(1 mark)*

> - **Malicious Insider** — Deliberate sabotage for revenge or profit. *Example:* Vitek Boden (Maroochy, 2000). *(0.5 mark)*
> - **Negligent Insider** — Unintentional harm through poor cyber hygiene. *Example:* Contractor at Davis-Besse Nuclear Plant (2003) introduced the Slammer worm via an infected laptop. *(0.5 mark)*
> - **Compromised Insider** — External attacker controlling an insider's credentials. *Example:* Spear-phished engineer whose credentials were used in TRITON/TRISIS (2017). *(0.5 mark)*

> **KTU Bonus Point:** Always state that insider threats account for nearly 60% of ICS security incidents (per IBM Cost of Insider Threats Report), making them the most underrated yet most damaging vector.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Students describe *only* IT-centric threats (generic viruses, generic phishing) without showing the **ICS-specific dimension** (e.g., PLC reprogramming, HMI spoofing, SIS targeting). Examiners deduct 2–3 marks.
> - **Common mistake 2:** For case studies, students write **only the name and date** without explaining the **attack mechanism, vector, impact, and lesson learned**. Examiners deduct up to 4 marks.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Students fail to **map threats to frameworks** (NIST, MITRE ATT&CK, IEC 62443). Always name-drop the framework — it gives a "free" 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Diagrams are skipped or drawn in plain ASCII without node labels. Always include at least **one labeled block diagram**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the **SANS ICS Kill Chain**. Discuss how it differs from the traditional Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Explain in detail the **Stuxnet** cyber-physical attack on the Iranian Natanz facility. Cover the attack vector, payload, propagation mechanism, target component, and the lesson learned for ICS security. **(7 marks)***

### Model Answer — Question A

**(a) SANS ICS Kill Chain vs Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain (7 marks):**

The **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain** (2011) was originally designed for IT networks and consists of seven sequential stages:

1. Reconnaissance
2. Weaponisation
3. Delivery
4. Exploitation
5. Installation
6. Command & Control
7. Actions on Objectives

It assumes a relatively linear, time-bounded intrusion, primarily aimed at data theft or IT disruption.

The **SANS ICS Kill Chain** (2015) explicitly recognises that **industrial attacks are two-stage** — they must first breach the IT environment and then pivot into OT to affect physical processes. *(1 mark for framework awareness)*

**Two Stages of the SANS ICS Kill Chain:**

**Stage 1 — IT Compromise** *(2 marks)*

| Step | IT-Network Action | ICS-Specific Implication |
|------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Reconnaissance | OSINT, Shodan | Identify PLC brand, HMI vendor |
| Weaponisation | Build ICS-aware malware | Custom PLC ladder logic |
| Delivery | Spear-phish engineer | Engineer holds OT credentials |
| Exploitation | RDP/HMI software CVE | Engineering workstation compromised |
| Installation | RAT, persistence | Long dwell time (months) |

**Stage 2 — OT Lateral Movement & Action** *(2 marks)*

| Step | OT-Network Action | ICS Consequence |
|------|-------------------|------------------|
| IT-to-OT Pivot | Compromise historian / jump host | Bridge to Level 2 DMZ |
| OT Reconnaissance | Scan Modbus/TCP, S7Comm, DNP3 | Map PLCs, RTUs, IEDs |
| Develop ICS Module | Code process-manipulation payload | Sabotage or ransom tool |
| Damage / Disruption | Alter PLC logic, spoof HMI | Physical process corruption |

**Key Differences (Final 2 marks):**

| Aspect | Lockheed Martin | SANS ICS |
|--------|----------------|----------|
| Scope | IT only | IT + OT |
| Time horizon | Days | Months / years |
| Objective | Data theft | Physical manipulation |
| Lateral movement | Optional | Mandatory second stage |
| Detection difficulty | Moderate | Very high in OT |

**(b) Stuxnet Case Study (7 marks):**

**Background** *(1 mark)*: Discovered in **June 2010**, Stuxnet is widely attributed to **US (NSA) and Israel (Unit 8200)** under Operation "Olympic Games." Target: **Natanz Uranium Enrichment Facility, Iran.**

**Attack Vector** *(1 mark)*: **Removable USB media** — initial infection of an engineer's laptop via a contractor's USB drive. Iran believed Natanz was air-gapped, but human-mediated bridging defeated the gap.

**Propagation Mechanism** *(1 mark)*: Stuxnet used **four zero-day exploits** (CVE-2010-2568 LNK, CVE-2010-2729 print spooler, CVE-2010-3338 elevation, CVE-2010-3888 task scheduler), making it the most sophisticated worm at the time. It spread via LAN, USB, and Siemens WinCC/STEP7 project files.

**Target Component** *(1 mark)*: **Siemens SIMATIC S7-315** and **S7-417 PLCs** controlling **IR-1 and IR-2m uranium centrifuges**. Frequency converters (Vacon, Fararo Paya) were manipulated via Profibus.

**Payload** *(2 marks)*:
1. **Recording phase:** Stuxnet recorded normal centrifuge spin frequency (1,064 Hz) for weeks.
2. **Attack phase:** It periodically raised rotor speed to **1,410 Hz** (resonant destructive frequency) for ~15 minutes, then dropped to **2 Hz** (stall frequency) for ~50 minutes, intermittently cycling to destroy rotor bearings.
3. **Operator deception:** Simultaneously, Stuxnet replayed pre-recorded "normal" HMI video feeds on operator screens so engineers saw no anomaly.

**Impact** *(0.5 mark)*: Approximately **1,000 of 5,000 centrifuges** destroyed; Iran's nuclear programme reportedly set back **1–2 years**.

**Lessons Learned for ICS Security** *(0.5 mark)*:
- **Air gaps do not work** — humans carry infections across.
- **Operator displays must be cryptographically authenticated** against the actual physical process state.
- **Asset inventories and whitelisting** are essential (Stuxnet's whitelist checked for Siemens Step 7 before activating payload — defensive lesson is to detect signature).
- **Supply-chain trust** cannot be implicit.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *Explain the **three categories of insider threats** in ICS. Using the equation **P_insider = I × C_access × (1 − M)**, calculate the probability of an insider incident for a case where Intent = 0.8, Capability/Access = 0.9, and Monitoring effectiveness = 0.2. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *With a causal-chain diagram, explain the **TRITON/TRISIS** attack of 2017. Why is this incident considered a **"paradigm shift"** in ICS cyber threats? **(7 marks)***

### Model Answer — Question B

**(a) Three Insider-Threat Categories + Risk Calculation (7 marks):**

**Three Categories** *(3 marks — 1 each)*:

1. **Malicious Insider** — Deliberate, motivated by revenge, financial gain, or ideology. The actor willingly causes harm. *Example:* Vitek Boden (Maroochy Shire, 2000).

2. **Negligent Insider** — Well-intentioned but careless. Causes harm through policy violations, weak passwords, unpatched systems, or accidental mishandling of removable media. *Example:* Davis-Besse Nuclear Plant contractor (Slammer worm, 2003).

3. **Compromised Insider** — A legitimate user whose account/device is hijacked by an external threat actor through phishing, credential dumps, or malware. The insider is unaware. *Example:* TRITON attack chain — engineers' credentials compromised in Saudi Arabia (2017).

**CERT/CMU-SEI Insider Threat Equation:** *(1 mark for stating the formula)*

$$
P_{\text{insider}} = I \cdot C_{\text{access}} \cdot (1 - M)
$$

**Given values:** *(1 mark)*

$$
I = 0.8,\quad C_{\text{access}} = 0.9,\quad M = 0.2
$$

**Step-by-step calculation:** *(1 mark)*

$$
1 - M = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8
$$

$$
P_{\text{insider}} = 0.8 \times 0.9 \times 0.8
$$

$$
P_{\text{insider}} = 0.8 \times 0.72 = 0.576
$$

**Conclusion / Comment** *(1 mark)*: The probability $P_{\text{insider}} = 0.576$ (57.6 %) is **high**, indicating a significant insider risk. The result shows that even when monitoring is non-zero (M = 0.2), if intent and access are high, residual insider risk remains substantial. **Engineering mitigation:** Deploy zero-trust architecture, behavioural User & Entity Behaviour Analytics (UEBA), least-privilege controls, periodic access review, and dual-control approval for critical PLC programming changes to increase $M$ towards 1.0.

---

**(b) TRITON/TRISIS — The Paradigm-Shift Attack (7 marks):**

**Overview** *(1 mark)*: The TRITON malware (also called TRISIS or HatMan), discovered in **December 2017** by Mandiant and Schneider Electric, targeted the **Triconex Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** controllers manufactured by Schneider Electric (model **Tricon MP3008**). The victim was a Saudi Arabian petrochemical facility.

**Attack Anatomy** *(3 marks)*:

1. **Initial compromise:** Spear-phishing of a plant engineer → remote access workstation infected.
2. **Lateral movement:** Through the plant DMZ, the attacker reached the engineering workstation of the Triconex SIS.
3. **Reconnaissance:** Tricon firmware version (10.0–10.4) was identified.
4. **Payload deployment:** A custom Python-based framework ("TRITON") communicated using the proprietary **TriStation** protocol. It reprogrammed the safety PLC logic.
5. **SIS state change:** The Triconex controllers were placed into a **"safe shutdown"** state, momentarily disabling the last line of automated safety defence.
6. **Premature trigger:** A likely coding error caused the SIS to enter an unexpected shutdown, alerting operators and revealing the attack — saving the plant from a deliberate catastrophe.

**Causal Chain Diagram:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\text{Phish} \rightarrow \text{WS Compromise} \rightarrow \text{DMZ Pivot} \rightarrow \text{Triconex Reprogram} \rightarrow \text{SIS Disabled} \rightarrow \text{Accidental Shutdown Reveals Attack}
$$

**Why TRITON is a Paradigm Shift** *(2 marks)*:

| Reason | Significance |
|--------|--------------|
| **First-ever malware targeting a Safety Instrumented System** | SIS is the *last* barrier between a process upset and a catastrophic accident (fire, explosion, toxic release). All prior ICS malware (Stuxnet, BlackEnergy, Industroyer) targeted *control* systems, not safety systems. |
| **Targets safety, not production** | Even if production is offline, TRITON aims to **eliminate automated safety response**, meaning a subsequent physical fault would go unmitigated. |
| **Demonstrates SIS-aware adversaries** | Attackers are now capable of writing code that understands IEC 61508 SIL-rated controllers — a level of OT expertise previously theoretical. |
| **Near-miss outcome** | TRITON is widely interpreted as an attempted **mass-casualty event**; its accidental trigger may have averted a Bhopal-scale disaster. |

**Lessons** *(for full marks — 1 additional point)*: TRITON mandates that **Safety Systems must be on isolated, monitored networks (Purdue Level 1 with hardened segmentation)**; SIS programming changes must require **two-person authentication** and **fingerprint / checksum verification** of logic; organisations must integrate **SIS into the SOC** (Security Operations Centre) for anomaly monitoring.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist — print / screenshot before exam.**

### ✅ The Must-Know Lists
- [ ] **Four primary threat actor types:** Nation-State APT, Organised Cybercrime, Insider, Hacktivist.
- [ ] **Four primary ICS threat vectors:** Spear-Phishing, Removable Media (USB), Remote Access (RDP/VPN), Supply Chain.
- [ ] **Four must-know malware families:** Stuxnet, BlackEnergy, Industroyer, TRITON.
- [ ] **Four must-know ransomware incidents in OT:** WannaCry (2017), NotPetya (2017), Colonial Pipeline (2021), Oldsmar Water (2021).
- [ ] **Three insider categories:** Malicious, Negligent, Compromised.
- [ ] **Two kill chains:** Lockheed Martin (IT, 7-stage) and SANS ICS (IT-then-OT, two-stage).

### ✅ The Must-Know Equations
- [ ] $R_{i,j} = \Theta_j \cdot V_i \cdot (1 - E_i)$ — Risk score per asset-threat pair.
- [ ] $P_{\text{insider}} = I \cdot C_{\text{access}} \cdot (1 - M)$ — Insider incident probability.
- [ ] $\mathbb{E}[\text{Payoff}]_P = P_{\text{success}} \cdot V_{\text{asset}} - C_{\text{attack}}$ — Attacker economics.

### ✅ The Must-Know Frameworks
- [ ] **IEC 62443** priority order: Safety → Availability → Integrity → Confidentiality.
- [ ] **NIST SP 800-30** — Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact.
- [ ] **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** — 11 tactic categories.
- [ ] **Purdue Model** — Levels 0 to 5.

### ✅ The Must-Know Case-Study Key Facts
- [ ] **Stuxnet (2010)** — Iran Natanz, Siemens S7-300, 4 zero-days, ~1000 centrifuges, 1064 Hz → 1410 Hz → 2 Hz.
- [ ] **BlackEnergy 3 (2015)** — First confirmed cyber-blackout, Ukraine, 225,000 customers, 6 h outage.
- [ ] **TRITON (2017)** — Saudi, Triconex SIS, paradigm shift (safety-targeting).
- [ ] **Colonial Pipeline (2021)** — DarkSide RaaS, $4.4M ransom, 6-day shutdown, OT not directly hit.

### ✅ The Must-Know "One-Liners"
- [ ] **Air gaps don't work** — humans carry infections.
- [ ] **ICS priorities differ from IT** — Safety first, Confidentiality last.
- [ ] **Insider threats = 60% of incidents** (CERT).
- [ ] **TRITON = first SIS-targeted malware.**
- [ ] **Stuxnet = first cyber-physical weapon.**
- [ ] **NotPetya = most destructive collateral wiper.**
- [ ] **RaaS = democratisation of ransomware.**
- [ ] **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS = standard OT adversary taxonomy.**

### ✅ Last-Minute Memory Hooks
- **Stuxnet → 1410 Hz** (the number sticks).
- **TRITON → Safety** (the paradigm shift).
- **BlackEnergy → 2015 → Ukraine → Blackout** (the year-country-event chain).
- **Colonial → $4.4M → 6 days** (the loss-duration).
- **Insider = I × C × (1 − M)** (the multiplier).

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Industrial Control System (ICS) cyber attack** is a deliberate, adversarial exploitation of digital, network, or physical vulnerabilities within the cyber–physical infrastructure that governs an industrial process, with the intent to disrupt, degrade, deny, manipulate, or destroy the Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (the **CIA Triad** re-mapped to industrial priorities of **Safety, Reliability, and Productivity**) of operational technology (OT) assets such as **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) systems**, **Distributed Control Systems (DCS)**, **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)**, **Remote Terminal Units (RTUs)**, and **Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** In the context of PEICT742, a cyber attack on industrial automation is **not** merely an IT data breach. It is a **safety-critical, cyber-physical event** where the consequences manifest in the physical world — pressure surges, valve mis-operations, chemical releases, equipment damage, and loss of human life. The **ISA/IEC 62443** standard series is the governing reference framework for industrial cybersecurity.

## 1.2 Core Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Full Form | Industrial Context |
|------|-----------|-------------------|
| **ICS** | Industrial Control System | Umbrella term for all control systems used in industry |
| **SCADA** | Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition | Geographically dispersed assets (pipelines, power grids) |
| **DCS** | Distributed Control System | Locally concentrated plant-wide control (refineries) |
| **PLC** | Programmable Logic Controller | Ruggedised computer executing ladder logic |
| **RTU** | Remote Terminal Unit | Telemetry device at remote field sites |
| **HMI** | Human-Machine Interface | Operator screen for visualising and commanding the process |
| **OT** | Operational Technology | Hardware/software that monitors/controls physical processes |
| **IT** | Information Technology | Business data, email, ERP systems |
| **Purdue Model** | Perdue Reference Architecture | 6-layer logical model separating IT from OT |
| **CVE** | Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures | Public catalogue of known security flaws |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital ICU

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of an industrial plant as a **hospital Intensive Care Unit (ICU)** for a giant machine.
>
> - The **PLCs** are the **heart-lung machine and infusion pumps** that keep the patient alive with exact chemical/electrical flow.
> - The **SCADA/DCS** is the **nurse monitoring station** showing all vitals on screens (HMI).
> - The **field sensors** (temperature, pressure, flow) are the **medical sensors** on the body.
> - The **engineering workstation** is the **doctor's tablet** used to change drug dosages.
>
> A **cyber attack** on this ICU is equivalent to a **malicious actor** quietly changing the drug dosage on the infusion pump, falsifying the heart-rate reading on the monitor, and locking the nurse out of the alarm panel. The patient (the plant) may be alive one minute and in cardiac arrest the next — and the nurse (operator) sees a *normal* screen. This is the terrifying nature of ICS attacks: **silent, invisible, and physically destructive**.

## 1.4 What Makes ICS Cyber Attacks Different from IT Attacks?

| Dimension | Traditional IT Attack | ICS Cyber Attack |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------|
| Primary Goal | Steal data, financial gain, espionage | Disrupt process, cause physical damage, harm people |
| Confidentiality Priority | **High** (data is the asset) | **Low** (process state is the asset) |
| Integrity Priority | Medium | **Highest** (tampered sensor reading = explosion) |
| Availability Priority | High (DDoS extortion) | **Highest** (downtime = revenue/catastrophic loss) |
| Tolerable Downtime | Hours to days | **Milliseconds to minutes** (safety interlocks) |
| Patching Cycle | Weekly/Monthly | **Months to years** (process must never stop) |
| System Lifetime | 3–5 years | **15–30 years** (legacy equipment) |
| Network Architecture | Client-Server, Cloud | **Deterministic, real-time fieldbus** |
| Consequences | Data leak, financial loss | **Loss of life, environmental disaster** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Cyber Kill Chain Applied to Industrial Systems

The **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain** is the canonical 7-stage model for understanding the anatomy of a cyber attack. In OT/ICS environments, the kill chain is stretched across **months to years** of reconnaissance because industrial attackers perform deep **process engineering study** before striking.

### Stage-by-Stage Breakdown

1. **Reconnaissance (IT-OT Boundary)**
   - *Why:* Attacker maps the corporate IT network to find the bridge to OT.
   - *How:* OSINT, Shodan searches for exposed HMIs, vendor brochures revealing PLC models, LinkedIn scraping of plant engineers.
   - *OT Twist:* Even a single published HMI screenshot reveals the **PLCMaker, firmware version, tag database structure**, and **process flow** — a goldmine for attack design.

2. **Weaponisation**
   - *Why:* Build a payload that survives the OT environment (no internet, no antivirus updates).
   - *How:* Craft spear-phishing emails with malicious attachments targeting engineering staff; prepare ICS-specific malware (e.g., **Stuxnet-style** `.lnk` files, **TRITON** TriStation protocol exploits).
   - *OT Twist:* Payloads are designed to **manipulate process variables, not just data**.

3. **Delivery**
   - *Why:* Get code into the OT enclave.
   - *How:* Phishing, infected USB drives carried by maintenance contractors, supply-chain compromise (HVAC vendor), watering-hole attacks on industry forums.
   - *OT Twist:* **Air-gapped myth debunked** — 90%+ of supposedly air-gapped ICS networks have at least one transient connection (laptop, vendor modem, remote support).

4. **Exploitation**
   - *Why:* Execute code on the engineering workstation or HMI.
   - *How:* Exploit CVE-listed flaws in Windows 7 (still common in HMI stations), use default credentials, abuse unauthenticated ICS protocols (Modbus, DNP3, Siemens S7).
   - *OT Twist:* **Lack of authentication** in legacy ICS protocols means *any* packet that reaches the fieldbus can issue a control command.

5. **Installation**
   - *Why:* Establish persistence so a reboot does not erase the attacker.
   - *How:* Modify PLC ladder logic, install rootkits on HMI, register a malicious service on the historian.
   - *OT Twist:* Tampering with the **PLC program (e.g., Stuxnet's .s7p project files)** is the ICS equivalent of rewiring a circuit breaker panel.

6. **Command and Control (C2)**
   - *Why:* Receive instructions and exfiltrate process data.
   - *How:* Modbus TCP covert channel, DNS tunneling, scheduled dial-out through the engineering modem.
   - *OT Twist:* C2 channels are **stealthy and slow** to avoid tripping anomaly detection.

7. **Actions on Objectives**
   - *Why:* Execute the physical attack.
   - *How:* Manipulate setpoints, spoof sensor readings, trigger unsafe sequences, sabotage safety instrumented systems (SIS).
   - *OT Twist:* This is the **cyber-to-physical transition** — packets become pressure, voltage, and chemical reactions.

## 2.2 Impact of Cyber Attacks on Industrial Operations and Safety

### 2.2.1 Operational Impact Categories

| Impact Category | Description | Industrial Example |
|-----------------|-------------|--------------------|
| **Process Disruption** | Unscheduled shutdown of production line | Sugar refinery boiler trip, refinery unit shut-down |
| **Production Loss** | Reduced throughput, off-spec product | Beverage plant produces wrong pH product for 8 hours |
| **Equipment Damage** | Physical destruction of machinery | Centrifuge spun to disintegration, motor bearings seized |
| **Environmental Release** | Toxic/flammable material escapes | Chemical plant stack emission violation |
| **Safety Incident** | Injury or fatality to personnel | Stuxnet-style centrifuge rupture injuring technician |
| **Financial Loss** | Direct cost + reputational + regulatory fines | Norsk Hydro (2018) — $71M in damages |
| **Data Exfiltration** | Loss of proprietary process know-how | Recipe theft, bidding data, IP leakage |
| **Cascading Failure** | One event triggers downstream grid/network collapse | Ukrainian power grid 2015 — 225,000 customers blacked out |

### 2.2.2 The Safety-Cyber Coupling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** A safety instrumented system (SIS) operates on the principle of **independent protection layers (IPL)**. The **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** rating (SIL 1, SIL 2, SIL 3, SIL 4 per **IEC 61508**) defines the probability of failure on demand (PFD). A cyber attack that disables the SIS bypasses the highest-priority IPL — the **last line of defence between the process and a catastrophe**.

The **Safety–Cyber Risk Equation** (from IEC 62443-3-2) is:

$$R_{\text{cyber-safety}} = T \times V \times C \times P_{\text{attack}} \times P_{\text{SIS defeat}}$$

Where:
- $T$ = Threat capability (skill, resources of adversary)
- $V$ = Vulnerability exposure (open ports, unpatched CVEs)
- $C$ = Consequence severity (human, environmental, financial)
- $P_{\text{attack}}$ = Probability of attack attempt
- $P_{\text{SIS defeat}}$ = Probability of successfully defeating the Safety Instrumented System

> [!WARNING]
> **Trap for KTU Students:** Do **not** confuse **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** with **Security Level (SL)**. SIL is a measure of *functional safety* (probability of dangerous failure), while SL (Target Security Level in IEC 62443-3-3) is a measure of *cyber security assurance*. They are **complementary but distinct**.

### 2.2.3 The Bow-Tie Model for Cyber-Induced Safety Events

The industrial safety community uses the **Bow-Tie Model** to link threats to consequences via preventive and mitigative barriers. A cyber attack **attacks the barriers themselves**, fundamentally changing the risk profile.

$$P(\text{Catastrophe}) = \frac{P(\text{Hazard initiated})}{P(\text{Preventive barrier works}) \times P(\text{Mitigative barrier works})}$$

If a cyber attack degrades the probability of the **SIS** barrier working from $0.999$ to $0.5$, the catastrophic event probability increases by orders of magnitude.

## 2.3 Security Challenges and Vulnerabilities in ICS

### 2.3.1 The Top 10 ICS-Specific Vulnerabilities (Compiled from CISA, ICS-CERT, and NIST SP 800-82)

1. **Insecure by Design Protocols** — Modbus, DNP3, Profibus, EtherNet/IP, BACnet, Siemens S7 were designed in the 1970s–80s **before cybersecurity was a concern**.
2. **Lack of Authentication** — Most fieldbus protocols have no user/password concept; any node on the wire is trusted.
3. **Unencrypted Communications** — Plain-text telemetry, plain-text setpoints, plain-text credentials.
4. **Default and Hardcoded Credentials** — Vendor-shipped PLCs often have `admin/admin`, `service/service`, or backdoor accounts.
5. **Insufficient Access Control** — No role-based access control on HMI; any operator can override interlocks.
6. **Incomplete Patch Management** — A PLC controlling a continuous chemical reactor cannot be rebooted during a campaign.
7. **Outdated Operating Systems** — Engineering workstations running **Windows XP, Windows 7, Windows Server 2003** because the SCADA vendor does not certify newer OS versions.
8. **Missing Network Segmentation** — Single flat Layer-2 network between corporate IT and field PLCs.
9. **Inadequate Logging and Monitoring** — No SIEM integration; PLC tag changes are not audited.
10. **Supply Chain Exposure** — Compromise at the vendor (SolarWinds-style) cascades into every customer.

### 2.3.2 Protocol-Level Vulnerability Map

| Protocol | Layer | Authentication | Encryption | Integrity | Common Vulnerabilities |
|----------|-------|----------------|------------|-----------|------------------------|
| **Modbus TCP (Port 502)** | App | **None** | **None** | **None** | Command injection, replay, MITM |
| **DNP3 Secure** | App | Optional (DNP3-SA) | Optional (AES-128) | Optional | Plain DNP3 has no security at all |
| **IEC 60870-5-104** | App | Optional | **None** by default | **None** | Clear-text setpoints, no replay protection |
| **IEC 61850 (MMS, GOOSE, SV)** | App | Limited | **GOOSE/SV unencrypted** for latency | Multicast, no signing | GOOSE message injection, SMV spoofing |
| **Profinet** | App | Limited | Optional | Optional | ARP spoofing, DCP manipulation |
| **EtherNet/IP (CIP)** | App | Optional (CIP Security) | Optional | Optional | Original CIP has none |
| **OPC Classic (DA/HDA)** | App | Windows DCOM | None | None | DCOM RPC exploits, lateral movement |
| **OPC UA** | App | **Built-in certificates** | **Built-in TLS** | **Built-in signing** | Misconfiguration, weak cert policies |
| **Siemens S7Comm** | App | Optional (S7CommPlus) | Optional | Optional | Stuxnet exploited plain S7 |
| **BACnet** | App | Optional | None | None | Building automation often wide open |

> [!NOTE]
> **The shift in industrial protocol security** is the migration from **Modbus/DNP3** to **OPC UA over TLS** and the IEC 62351 standard series. IEC 62351 specifically addresses cybersecurity for power-system communication (IEC 60870-5, IEC 61850, DNP3).

## 2.4 Human Factors — The Often-Ignored Attack Surface

### 2.4.1 Why Human Factors Dominate ICS Risk

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Industry surveys consistently show that **human factors contribute to 60–80% of ICS security incidents** — a figure that is significantly higher than in the IT world (where the figure is around 30–40%).

### 2.4.2 Human-Factor Challenges Unique to ICS

| Challenge | Description | Real-World Manifestation |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Always-On Operations** | Plant cannot stop for security maintenance | Patches postponed for years |
| **Operational Priority** | Availability trumps security | "Keep the line running at all costs" |
| **Legacy Skills Gap** | Engineers trained on hardware, not cybersecurity | Clicking through security warnings |
| **Vendor Trust Culture** | Maintenance contractors given broad access | USB stick from vendor = trust accepted |
| **Shift Handovers** | Multiple operators, multiple access points | Shared credentials, no accountability |
| **Alert Fatigue** | Operators ignore the 1000th "abnormal" alarm | Real attack blended into noise |
| **Cognitive Bias** | "It can't happen here" mentality | Underestimating ransomware impact |
| **Resistance to Change** | Established procedures seen as sacrosanct | Refusal to use password manager |
| **Physical Security Compromise** | Tailgating into control rooms | Attacker plants rogue device inside |

### 2.4.3 The Legacy System Conundrum

Industrial plants routinely operate **assets that are 20, 30, even 50 years old**. Examples include:
- **Sensors** with RS-232 serial ports (no IP, no patching path).
- **PLCs** with proprietary firmware from defunct vendors.
- **SCADA software** that runs only on Windows XP because the SCADA vendor went out of business in 2010.
- **Fieldbus networks** (Profibus, Foundation Fieldbus) with no encryption capability in the hardware.

The resulting **legacy tax** is summarised as:

$$\text{Legacy Risk} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\text{Age}_i \times \text{Criticality}_i \times \text{InternetExposure}_i)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "patch the legacy system" as the solution. The correct answer is to **compensate** for the legacy risk through **compensating controls** — network isolation via data diodes, unidirectional gateways, virtual patching, network segmentation, and physical security — not through direct patching.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Standard | Key Value / Definition |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------|
| CIA Triad (IT) | C, I, A | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability |
| ICS Priority Re-mapping | S, R, P | Safety, Reliability, Productivity |
| Purdue Model Layers | L0–L5 | Process → Control → Supervisory → Site Operations → DMZ → Enterprise |
| IEC 62443 Security Levels | SL 1–4 | Casual → Intentional → Sophisticated → Nation-state |
| SIL (IEC 61508) | SIL 1–4 | PFD ranges from $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$ |
| Mean Time to Compromise | MTTC | Time for attacker to breach (lower is worse) |
| Mean Time to Detect | MTTD | Time for defender to notice (lower is better) |
| Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures | CVE | MITRE-assigned vulnerability ID |
| Industrial Demilitarised Zone | IDMZ | Conduit layer between IT and OT |
| Unidirectional Security Gateway | USG | Hardware enforcing one-way data flow |
| Safety Instrumented System | SIS | Independent protection layer for process safety |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Matrices

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Risk Score for an ICS Asset

The **CVSS-inspired ICS risk score** is computed as follows. We derive it step by step from first principles.

### Step 1: Identify the Asset and its Criticality

Each industrial asset (PLC, HMI, server) is assigned a **criticality score** $C$ based on its function:

$$C = w_1 \cdot S_{\text{safety}} + w_2 \cdot S_{\text{env}} + w_3 \cdot S_{\text{prod}} + w_4 \cdot S_{\text{reg}}$$

Where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1.0$ and each $S$ is rated 1–5.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** A PLC controlling a hydrocracker in a refinery:
> $S_{\text{safety}} = 5$ (high-pressure hydrogen, explosive), $S_{\text{env}} = 4$ (hydrocarbon release), $S_{\text{prod}} = 5$ (revenue-critical), $S_{\text{reg}} = 4$ (OSHA, EPA).
> With weights $w = (0.4, 0.2, 0.3, 0.1)$:
> $C = 0.4(5) + 0.2(4) + 0.3(5) + 0.1(4) = 2.0 + 0.8 + 1.5 + 0.4 = 4.7$

### Step 2: Identify Vulnerabilities on the Asset

Each **CVE** has a **CVSS base score** $V \in [0, 10]$. The aggregate vulnerability score is:

$$V_{\text{agg}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \cdot \text{Exploitability}_i}{n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** Two CVEs on the engineering workstation — CVE-2020-0796 (SMBGhost, $V = 10$) and CVE-2017-0144 (EternalBlue, $V = 8.1$) — both with high exploitability.
> $V_{\text{agg}} = \frac{10(1.0) + 8.1(0.9)}{2} = \frac{10 + 7.29}{2} = 8.65$

### Step 3: Estimate Threat Likelihood

The **threat likelihood** $T$ is the annualised probability of an attack attempt on this asset. Empirically:

$$T = T_{\text{baseline}} \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{k} f_j$$

Where $f_j$ are amplifying factors (e.g., $f_{\text{internet-facing}} = 3$, $f_{\text{legacy OS}} = 2$, $f_{\text{no segmentation}} = 1.5$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** Baseline $T_{\text{baseline}} = 0.05$ (5% per year). Asset is internet-facing on the SCADA DMZ ($f_1 = 3$), runs Windows 7 ($f_2 = 2$), shares a flat network with corporate IT ($f_3 = 1.5$).
> $T = 0.05 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1.5 = 0.45$ (45% per year).

### Step 4: Compute Composite Risk

$$R = C \cdot V_{\text{agg}} \cdot T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (continued):**
> $R = 4.7 \cdot 8.65 \cdot 0.45 = 18.29$
>
> A risk score above **15** is classified as **CRITICAL** on the KTU-aligned risk matrix, demanding immediate remediation through compensating controls.

## 3.2 Risk Matrix — KTU-Approved Mapping

| Risk Score $R$ | Classification | Required Action |
|----------------|----------------|-----------------|
| $0 \le R < 3$ | **Low** | Accept, monitor annually |
| $3 \le R < 7$ | **Medium** | Plan remediation within 12 months |
| $7 \le R < 15$ | **High** | Remediate within 90 days, compensating controls |
| $R \ge 15$ | **Critical** | Immediate isolation + emergency response |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Vulnerability × Consequence Mapping

| Vulnerability Class \ Consequence | Process Shutdown | Equipment Damage | Environmental Release | Safety Incident |
|-----------------------------------|------------------|------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **Insecure Protocol (Modbus)** | **High** | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| **No Authentication** | **High** | **High** | **High** | **High** |
| **Unpatched Windows HMI** | **High** | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Default Credentials** | Medium | **High** | **High** | **High** |
| **Flat Network** | **High** | **High** | **High** | **High** |
| **Phishing of Engineer** | **High** | Medium | Medium | Low |
| **Rogue USB** | Medium | **High** | **High** | **High** |
| **Vendor Remote Access** | **High** | **High** | **High** | Medium |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Bow-Tie Probability

**Problem:** A reactor has a hazard (over-pressurisation) occurring with probability $P(H) = 10^{-3}$ per year. The preventive barrier (pressure control loop) works with probability $P(B_p) = 0.99$, and the mitigative barrier (SIS relief valve) works with probability $P(B_m) = 0.999$. Calculate the baseline catastrophe probability.

### Step 1: Write the Bow-Tie formula

$$P(\text{catastrophe}) = P(H) \cdot (1 - P(B_p)) \cdot (1 - P(B_m))$$

### Step 2: Substitute values

$$P(\text{catastrophe}) = 10^{-3} \cdot (1 - 0.99) \cdot (1 - 0.999)$$

### Step 3: Compute

$$P(\text{catastrophe}) = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.01 \cdot 0.001 = 10^{-8} \text{ per year}$$

### Step 4: Now, model a cyber attack that defeats the SIS

The attacker reduces $P(B_m)$ from $0.999$ to $0.1$ (90% reduction in SIS effectiveness):

$$P(\text{catastrophe}}^\text{cyber}) = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.01 \cdot (1 - 0.1) = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.01 \cdot 0.9 = 9 \times 10^{-6}$$

### Step 5: Compare the risk increase

$$\text{Risk Multiplier} = \frac{P_{\text{cyber}}}{P_{\text{baseline}}} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-8}} = 900$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A single cyber-induced degradation of the SIS effectiveness increases the catastrophe probability by a factor of **900** — a three-orders-of-magnitude increase. This is why **cyber-security of the SIS** is a non-negotiable requirement in IEC 62443.

---
