# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> Explain the **CIA Triad** and state how its priorities are re-mapped in the context of an **Industrial Control System (ICS)**. Why is **availability** the paramount concern in an ICS unlike in a traditional IT system?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **CIA Triad** in information security consists of **Confidentiality** (no unauthorised disclosure), **Integrity** (no unauthorised modification), and **Availability** (no interruption of service).

In an **ICS**, the priorities are re-mapped to:
1. **Safety** (human life, environmental protection) — top priority
2. **Reliability / Availability** (continuous plant operation) — high priority
3. **Integrity** (correct process data and commands) — high priority
4. **Confidentiality** (process secrets) — low priority

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** (1 mark CIA definition, 1 mark re-mapping, 1 mark availability justification). Availability dominates in ICS because unscheduled downtime translates to **direct revenue loss**, **safety risk** (e.g., blocked cooling), and **environmental non-compliance**. A 1-hour outage in a petrochemical plant can cost **$500,000** in lost production and trigger regulatory penalties.

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Differentiate between **IT (Information Technology)** and **OT (Operational Technology)** systems. List any **four security challenges** that are unique to OT environments.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**IT** systems handle business data (email, ERP, finance) on general-purpose computing platforms. **OT** systems monitor and control physical industrial processes via PLCs, SCADA, DCS, and field sensors.

**Four unique OT security challenges:**
1. **Legacy systems** — 20+ year-old assets with no patch path.
2. **Always-on operation** — patching requires shutdown that is rarely permitted.
3. **Proprietary protocols** — Modbus, DNP3, Profibus have no built-in security.
4. **Safety-critical coupling** — a cyber failure can cause physical harm.
5. (Bonus) **Deterministic real-time constraints** — security scanning can disrupt process timing.
6. (Bonus) **Long asset lifetime** — 15–30 years vs. 3–5 years for IT.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** (1 mark definition of IT/OT, 2 marks for four challenges with brief justification).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram of the **Purdue Reference Model**, explain each layer from L0 to L5 and identify the **attack surfaces** that exist at each layer in a typical industrial plant. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **specific vulnerabilities of ICS protocols** (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, Siemens S7) and explain how the **IEC 62351** standard series addresses these shortcomings. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Purdue Reference Model (7 Marks)**

The Purdue Reference Model (also called the **Purdue Enterprise Reference Architecture** or **PERA**) is a six-layer logical model that segments enterprise and industrial networks.

- **Layer 0 — Physical Process:** The actual plant equipment — pipes, pumps, motors, valves, reactors, turbines. The physical phenomenon being controlled.
- **Layer 1 — Basic Control:** Sensors (temperature, pressure, flow, level), actuators, drives. The interface between the digital control system and the physical process.
- **Layer 2 — Area Supervisory Control:** PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers. These execute closed-loop control algorithms (PID, ladder logic, function block).
- **Layer 3 — Site Operations:** SCADA servers, HMIs, engineering workstations, historians. Operators monitor the plant here.
- **Layer 3.5 — Industrial DMZ:** A buffer zone between IT and OT containing the historian mirror, antivirus update server, jump host, and identity server.
- **Layer 4 — Site Business Planning:** Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES), laboratory information systems.
- **Layer 5 — Enterprise Network:** Corporate IT — email, ERP, internet access.

> **Attack Surfaces per Layer:**
> - L0: Physical tampering, sensor spoofing, cable tapping.
> - L1: Firmware tampering, sensor spoofing, hardware implant.
> - L2: PLC ladder logic injection, default credentials, unauthenticated commands.
> - L3: HMI phishing, ransomware on engineering workstation, USB-borne malware.
> - L3.5: IDMZ misconfiguration, lateral movement from IT.
> - L4: MES database compromise, ERP-IT pivot.
> - L5: Phishing, web-borne malware entering through corporate users.

(3 marks for layer definitions, 2 marks for diagram explanation, 2 marks for attack surface identification.)

**(b) ICS Protocol Vulnerabilities and IEC 62351 (7 Marks)**

| Protocol | Vulnerability |
|----------|---------------|
| **Modbus TCP** | No authentication, no encryption, no integrity. A packet claiming to be a "write coil" command is accepted by any slave. Replay attacks trivial. |
| **DNP3 (plain)** | No authentication. Broadcast messages can inject false commands. Used widely in water/wastewater and electric utilities. |
| **IEC 60870-5-104** | Plain-text setpoints over TCP. No replay protection in the base standard. |
| **Siemens S7Comm** | Stuxnet exploited the S7Comm protocol to reprogram S7-300/400 PLCs by injecting modified ladder logic blocks. |

> **IEC 62351** is the cybersecurity standard for power-system communication. It applies security to protocols that were never designed with security in mind:
> - **Authentication** of messages (digital signatures).
> - **Encryption** of payloads (where latency permits).
> - **Replay protection** through sequence numbers and timestamps.
> - **Role-based access control** at the protocol layer.
> - Specific profiles for **IEC 60870-5-104** (Part 6), **DNP3** (Part 5), **IEC 61850** (Part 6/9), and **IEC 61970/61968** CIM (Part 7/8).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 62351 vs. IEC 62443:** Do not confuse them. **IEC 62351** is **protocol-specific** (cryptography for power protocols). **IEC 62443** is a **system-wide** cybersecurity framework covering people, process, and technology.

(3 marks for protocol vulnerabilities, 2 marks for attack examples, 2 marks for IEC 62351 features.)

---

### **Question B-B (Choice 2 — Alternative to B-A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the **cyber kill chain** as applied to **Industrial Control Systems**. Use a real-world ICS incident (e.g., **Stuxnet, BlackEnergy, TRITON, Industroyer, Colonial Pipeline**) to illustrate how the stages were executed. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Analyse the **human factor challenges** in securing ICS. Discuss why patching, segmentation, and authentication are operationally difficult in industrial plants, citing the **legacy systems** problem. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) ICS Cyber Kill Chain with Stuxnet Case Study (7 Marks)**

The **Cyber Kill Chain** consists of 7 stages. Mapping **Stuxnet** (2010, Iran nuclear enrichment):

1. **Reconnaissance:** Siemens STEP 7 software and S7-315/S7-417 PLC manuals published online gave attackers the documentation they needed.
2. **Weaponisation:** A multi-component worm exploiting **.lnk** file vulnerability (CVE-2010-2568) and **Printer Spooler** (MS10-061), plus four zero-days.
3. **Delivery:** Carried on **USB drives** belonging to contractors working at Natanz.
4. **Exploitation:** Auto-ran on Windows systems, propagated via SMB, then located Siemens PCS 7 / WinCC systems.
5. **Installation:** Modified the **PLC ladder logic (.s7p project files)** to vary rotor speeds between 807 Hz and 1210 Hz while spoofing normal readings to the HMI.
6. **Command and Control:** Stuxnet had a P2P update mechanism over the air-gapped network.
7. **Actions on Objectives:** Centrifuges destroyed by excessive spin — **~1,000 centrifuges damaged** — while operators saw "normal" telemetry.

> **Comparison with other incidents:**
> - **BlackEnergy (2015, Ukraine):** Spear-phished railway/energy IT staff, harvested VPN credentials, opened HMI breakers.
> - **TRITON (2017, Saudi Arabia):** Targeted Schneider Electric **Triconex SIS** controllers — the first attack on a safety system.
> - **Colonial Pipeline (2021):** DarkSide ransomware on the **billing IT system** led to precautionary pipeline shutdown — demonstrating the **IT-to-OT spillover** risk.

(3 marks for kill chain stages, 2 marks for Stuxnet, 2 marks for other incidents.)

**(b) Human Factor and Legacy System Challenges (7 Marks)**

| Challenge | Operational Reality | Cyber Consequence |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **24/7 Operation** | Plant revenue depends on continuous running; "downtime = loss" | Patches deferred for years; vulnerabilities accumulate |
| **Vendor Lock-in** | SCADA software requires specific certified OS version | Windows XP/7 remain in production for decades |
| **Workforce Skill Gap** | Plant engineers are mechanical/chemical specialists, not cybersecurity experts | Phishing emails are clicked, warnings ignored |
| **Procedural Inertia** | Established shift handovers use shared credentials | No individual accountability for cyber actions |
| **Physical-Access Culture** | Trust-based control room access; tailgating common | Rogue devices, social engineering trivial |
| **Remote Vendor Access** | Vendors need direct PLC access for support | Open RDP/VPN channels without MFA |
| **Incident Response** | "Cyber incident response" procedure often absent | Real attacks detected only after physical damage |

> **The Legacy Systems Trap:**
> Industrial plants contain **multi-generational assets** — a 1985 PLC, a 1998 SCADA, a 2008 historian, and a 2020 cloud dashboard — all interconnected. The **oldest asset** dictates the **weakest security posture** of the whole system. Removing or replacing legacy assets costs **millions of dollars** and requires a **complete process shutdown** of weeks/months — operationally unacceptable for most plants.

> **Compensating Controls** (instead of patching legacy):
> - **Network isolation** through unidirectional gateways (data diodes).
> - **Virtual patching** at the network layer.
> - **Microsegmentation** to contain blast radius.
> - **Behavioural monitoring** for anomaly detection.
> - **Strict access control** at protocol level.

(3 marks for human factor table, 2 marks for legacy analysis, 2 marks for compensating controls.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SIL (Safety Integrity Level) with SL (Security Level).** SIL is a *functional safety* metric (IEC 61508); SL is a *cybersecurity* metric (IEC 62443-3-3). Writing "SIL 4 means the system is unhackable" will cost full marks.
> 2. **Suggesting "install antivirus" as a complete ICS solution.** ICS workstations often cannot run real-time AV; signature updates break SCADA software. Markers look for *defence-in-depth* answers.
> 3. **Treating the IT/OT boundary as non-existent.** Saying "the SCADA is connected to the internet" without specifying **compensating controls** (DMZ, data diode, jump host) loses marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the Stuxnet specifics.** Generic answers like "malware caused damage" score poorly. The marker expects **PLC ladder-logic modification, frequency manipulation 807 Hz → 1410 Hz, HMI spoofing** — exact technical details.
> 5. **Forgetting the human factor.** A purely technical answer without discussing operators, engineers, vendor culture, and legacy systems is incomplete for 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Hand-waving "use secure protocols" without naming them.** Specify **OPC UA with security policies, IEC 62351, TLS 1.2+ with mutual authentication**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> **Module 3 — Rapid Recall (Read before exam):**
>
> - **CIA Triad vs ICS priorities:** C-I-A in IT becomes **Safety → Integrity → Availability → Confidentiality** in OT.
> - **Purdue Model:** L0 Process → L1 Sensors → L2 PLCs → L3 SCADA/HMI → L3.5 IDMZ → L4 MES → L5 Enterprise.
> - **Top 5 legacy ICS protocols are insecure:** Modbus TCP (port 502), DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, Siemens S7Comm, Profinet — all lack native auth, encryption, and integrity.
> - **IEC 62351** = protocol security; **IEC 62443** = system-wide cybersecurity framework; **IEC 61508** = functional safety (SIL).
> - **Cyber Kill Chain has 7 stages:** Recon → Weaponise → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Act.
> - **Stuxnet (2010)** is the iconic ICS attack: USB-delivered, targeted **Siemens S7 PLCs**, modified **centrifuge rotor frequency**, **spoofed HMI readings** — destroyed ~1000 centrifuges at Natanz.
> - **TRITON (2017)** was the first attack on a **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** — Schneider Triconex.
> - **Colonial Pipeline (2021)** shut down due to **IT ransomware** spillover, not direct OT compromise.
> - **Human factor = 60–80% of ICS incidents** (vs 30–40% in IT).
> - **Legacy systems (15–30 year lifetime)** force **compensating controls** (data diodes, micro-segmentation, virtual patching) — not direct patching.
> - **Air gap is a myth** — over 90% of supposedly air-gapped ICS have at least one transient connection (vendor modem, laptop, USB).
> - **Risk equation:** $R = C \times V \times T$ — **Criticality × Vulnerability × Threat** drives the asset risk score.
> - **Bow-Tie catastrophe multiplier** for cyber-defeated SIS can exceed **900× baseline risk** (worked example in §3.4).
> - **Always cite standards** in KTU answers: **ISA/IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-82, IEC 62351, IEC 61508, NERC-CIP, ISO 27019** to score full marks.
> - **Common KTU 3-markers** on this module: define ICS/SCADA, list CIA re-mapping, list 4 OT security challenges, name 2 vulnerable protocols, identify attack surface per Purdue layer.
> - **Common KTU 14-markers**: Purdue model + attack surfaces, protocol vulnerability analysis + IEC 62351, kill chain + Stuxnet, human factors + legacy.

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **ICS Risk Assessment (KTU 2024 Definition):** A systematic, multi-disciplinary process of identifying, estimating, and prioritizing risks to industrial control systems (ICS) and operational technology (OT) environments. It combines threat identification, vulnerability analysis, asset characterization, likelihood estimation, and consequence evaluation to produce a defensible risk register that supports mitigation, compliance (e.g., **IEC 62443-3-2**, **NIST SP 800-82**, and **NIST CSF**), and incident response planning.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, ICS Risk Management is treated as a **continuous lifecycle** rather than a one-time audit. The lifecycle comprises four interlocking pillars:

1. **Risk Identification** — what can go wrong?
2. **Risk Analysis** — how likely and how bad?
3. **Risk Evaluation** — is the risk acceptable?
4. **Risk Treatment / Mitigation** — what do we do about it?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Candidates must demonstrate working knowledge of the difference between *IT Risk* and *OT/ICS Risk*. Unlike IT systems, ICS prioritizes **safety, availability, and integrity** over confidentiality — a direct inversion of the classic CIA triad for enterprise systems.

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition Pump)

Imagine you are the **owner of a chemical plant** that produces industrial solvents. The plant has a control room (SCADA), PLCs controlling valves, sensors measuring pressure, and historians storing process data.

**Step 1 — Walk the Plant (Asset Identification):**
You walk through the plant with a clipboard and list everything that matters: the **PLC controlling the reactor**, the **pressure transmitter**, the **engineering workstation**, the **HMI in the control room**, and the **fibre link to the safety system (SIS)**. These are your *critical assets*.

**Step 2 — Find the Weak Spots (Vulnerability Identification):**
You notice: the PLC is still running on a **default password**, an old HMI is connected to the corporate network for "convenience", and a USB port on an engineering laptop is unrestricted. These are your *vulnerabilities*.

**Step 3 — Imagine What Could Happen (Threat Modelling):**
A disgruntled former employee, a ransomware group, a careless contractor, a natural flood. These are your *threats*.

**Step 4 — Calculate the Pain (Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Consequence):**
A ransomware attack on the engineering workstation that pivots to the SIS would cause **catastrophic** consequences — the reactor could overpressure, endangering lives. That's a *HIGH* risk. The same ransomware on a non-critical printer? That's *LOW*.

**Step 5 — Act (Mitigation & Response):**
You put the SIS on an isolated VLAN, enforce multi-factor authentication on remote access, whitelist USB devices, and create an incident playbook for ransomware. This entire journey is **ICS Risk Assessment & Management**.

---

## 1.3 Why Traditional IT Risk Models Fail in ICS

| Dimension | Enterprise IT | Industrial OT / ICS |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Confidentiality | **Availability & Safety** |
| Patch Cadence | Days to weeks | Months to years (planned shutdowns) |
| Downtime Tolerance | Hours | **Zero to seconds** (safety implications) |
| Network Architecture | Flat, cloud-friendly | **Hierarchical Purdue Model (Levels 0-5)** |
| Threat Actors | Hacktivists, financial criminals | Nation-states, insider sabotage, ransomware, supply-chain |
| System Lifecycle | 3-5 years | **15-25 years** |
| Anti-virus Feasibility | Standard | Often impossible (deterministic controllers) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do **not** apply pure IT risk frameworks (e.g., ISO 27005 alone) to ICS without adaptation. Use **IEC 62443-3-2** as the foundational zone-based methodology and overlay **NIST SP 800-82** for OT-specific guidance.

---

## 1.4 Core Terminology (KTU Glossary Standard)

- **Asset:** Anything of value to the industrial process (PLCs, HMIs, historians, field sensors, safety instrumented systems, intellectual property, and even *human operators*).
- **Threat:** Any circumstance or event with the potential to adversely impact operations (malicious, accidental, environmental).
- **Vulnerability:** A weakness in an asset, control, or process that a threat can exploit.
- **Likelihood:** The probability that a given threat will exercise a given vulnerability.
- **Consequence / Impact:** The severity of harm resulting from a successful exploitation event.
- **Risk:** A function of threat, vulnerability, likelihood, and consequence. Expressed as $R = f(T, V, L, C)$.
- **Risk Tolerance / Acceptance Criteria:** The level of risk an organization is willing to accept.
- **Residual Risk:** The risk remaining *after* mitigation controls are applied.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Risk Management Lifecycle (ISO 31000 + IEC 62443-3-2 Hybrid)

KTU examiners expect students to be able to **draw and explain** the lifecycle. The academically accepted version combines ISO 31000's process model with IEC 62443-3-2's ICS-specific zone/conduit thinking.

### Stage 1 — Establish Context
Define the **scope of the assessment**, identify the **risk owners**, document the **system under consideration (SUC)**, and establish the **risk acceptance criteria** (e.g., safety: zero, financial: < ₹10 lakh exposure, reputational: < 24-hour media cycle).

### Stage 2 — Asset Identification & Characterisation
Identify *what* must be protected. KTU 2024 emphasises a **four-tier classification**:

| Tier | Description | ICS Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1 — Safety** | Systems whose failure can cause loss of life or environmental harm | Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS), Emergency Shutdown (ESD), fire & gas detectors |
| **Tier 2 — Operational Criticality** | Systems whose failure causes major production loss or product quality issues | Reactor PLC, DCS controllers, historian servers |
| **Tier 3 — Business / Support** | Systems supporting plant operations but not directly controlling them | Engineering workstations, vendor remote access, printers |
| **Tier 4 — Ancillary** | Systems with low impact on the process | Office IT shared with plant, badge systems |

### Stage 3 — Threat Identification
Catalog threats using a structured taxonomy:

- **Adversarial Human:** Nation-state APTs (e.g., TRITON, INDUSTROYER), insider threats, hacktivists, ransomware crews.
- **Non-adversarial Human:** Untrained operator, accidental misconfiguration, careless contractor.
- **Environmental:** Flood, lightning, dust, temperature extremes, electromagnetic interference.
- **Technical:** Hardware failure, firmware bug, software vulnerability (e.g., unpatched Windows 7 HMI), protocol weakness (Modbus, DNP3 lack authentication).
- **Supply Chain:** Compromised vendor laptop, malicious firmware in a new PLC, counterfeit hardware.

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks: *"List five threat categories specific to an ICS."* Use the acronym **HATES**: **H**uman-adversarial, **A**ccidental, **T**echnical, **E**nvironmental, **S**upply-chain.

### Stage 4 — Vulnerability Identification
Sources of vulnerabilities in ICS include:

- **Inherent Protocol Weakness:** Modbus/TCP, DNP3, and Profinet have **no native authentication or encryption**.
- **Legacy Operating Systems:** Windows XP, Windows 7, embedded RTOS with no patch path.
- **Default / Hard-coded Credentials:** Engineering backdoors, vendor accounts (e.g., `admin/admin`).
- **Unrestricted Network Connectivity:** Flat Layer-2 networks, IT-OT bridges, remote access via TeamViewer/AnyDesk.
- **Insecure Remote Access:** Vendor modems, jump hosts, exposed RDP/VPN.
- **Inadequate Segmentation:** Mixing Levels 0-1 (field devices) with Level 4-5 (enterprise).
- **Insufficient Logging:** No SIEM in OT zones, no audit trail on configuration changes.
- **Physical Exposure:** Equipment in unlocked cabinets, USB ports accessible.

### Stage 5 — Risk Analysis (Likelihood × Consequence)

Risk is a composite of **Likelihood (L)** and **Consequence (C)**. Two accepted KTU-grade scoring schemes:

#### Quantitative (NIST SP 800-30 Style)

$$R = L \times C \times T$$

where $L$ is likelihood (probability 0-1), $C$ is consequence impact (monetary or operational units), and $T$ is the threat capability factor (0-1 representing adversary capability).

#### Semi-Quantitative (5×5 Matrix — KTU Board Favourite)

Likelihood and Consequence are scored 1-5, then:

$$R_{score} = L \times C$$

The product falls into a category:

| $R_{score}$ Range | Risk Level | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1-4 | **Low** | Accept, monitor |
| 5-9 | **Medium** | Mitigate within 6 months |
| 10-15 | **High** | Mitigate within 30 days |
| 16-25 | **Critical** | Immediate action / shutdown if needed |

### Stage 6 — Risk Evaluation & Treatment

Compare $R_{score}$ against the **risk acceptance criteria**. If $R_{score}$ exceeds tolerance, select a treatment:

1. **Avoidance** — eliminate the activity causing risk.
2. **Reduction (Mitigation)** — apply controls to reduce $L$ or $C$.
3. **Transfer (Sharing)** — cyber insurance, outsourcing, contractual clauses.
4. **Acceptance (Retention)** — formally document why residual risk is tolerated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After applying controls, **recalculate residual risk**. The KTU board explicitly tests this concept.

### Stage 7 — Communication, Monitoring & Review

Risk registers must be **living documents**, updated after every incident, architecture change, or new threat intelligence.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Units / Scale | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base Risk | $R = T \times V \times C$ | dimensionless score | Generic qualitative model |
| 2 | NIST Quantitative | $R = L \times C \times T$ | probability × impact | NIST SP 800-30 |
| 3 | Semi-Quantitative | $R = L \times C$ | score 1-25 | 5×5 matrix (KTU board standard) |
| 4 | Residual Risk | $R_{res} = R_{initial} - \Delta R_{controls}$ | score | Post-mitigation recalculation |
| 5 | Defence-in-Depth | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{block,i}$ | probability layers | Layered security model |
| 6 | Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR) | $MTTR = \frac{\sum t_{recovery}}{N_{incidents}}$ | hours / days | Incident response KPI |
| 7 | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) | $MTBF = \frac{\sum t_{operating}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Reliability metric |
| 8 | Recovery Time Objective (RTO) | $RTO \le T_{max-allowable}$ | hours | Maximum tolerable downtime |
| 9 | Recovery Point Objective (RPO) | $RPO \le T_{max-data-loss}$ | minutes / hours | Maximum tolerable data loss |
| 10 | Availability (Tier 1 ICS) | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$ | percentage | Target typically $\ge 99.99\%$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not** confuse *Availability* (uptime) with *Confidentiality*. In ICS, the order of priorities is **Safety → Availability → Integrity → Confidentiality**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Models

- **IEC 62443-3-2** is used by Siemens, Honeywell, and ABB to certify plant cyber readiness.
- **NIST CSF** is the de-facto US standard and the basis for KTU's question bank framing.
- **Bow-tie Analysis** (popular in oil & gas) visualises threats on the left, consequences on the right, with mitigations in the middle "knot" — extremely useful for KTU diagram questions.
- **Cyber PHAZOP / HAZOP-Cy** is a new discipline merging process safety HAZOP with cyber threat modelling.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: 5×5 Risk Matrix for a Refinery DCS

### Problem Setup

Consider a refinery with a Distributed Control System (DCS). The cybersecurity team identifies the following scenario:

> *"A contractor with remote VPN access downloads a malicious payload from a phishing email. The malware pivots from the engineering workstation to the DCS controller, manipulating the setpoint of a distillation column, causing off-spec product for 6 hours and a $200,000 production loss."*

Given:
- **Likelihood (L) = 3** (Possible — phishing is common, but pivot to DCS requires segmentation bypass)
- **Consequence (C) = 4** (Major — $200k loss, off-spec product, regulatory notification)

### Step 1 — Calculate Raw Risk Score

Using the semi-quantitative model:

$$R_{score} = L \times C$$

$$R_{score} = 3 \times 4 = 12$$

### Step 2 — Map to Risk Category

Looking up the risk band table:

$$12 \in [10, 15] \Rightarrow \textbf{High Risk}$$

### Step 3 — Determine Required Treatment

A *High* risk mandates mitigation within 30 days. Possible controls:

- **Technical:** Enforce MFA on VPN, apply application whitelisting on engineering workstation, segment DCS into Purdue Level 1-2 zone with a unidirectional gateway (data diode) at the boundary.
- **Administrative:** Phishing awareness training, contract clauses on USB use, least-privilege role for contractor.
- **Physical:** Locked engineering cabinets, badge access to control room.

### Step 4 — Recalculate Residual Risk Post-Control

Assume the engineering workstation is now whitelisted, MFA enforced, and the DCS is segmented:

- New Likelihood (L') = 1 (Rare — three independent controls must fail)
- New Consequence (C') = 2 (Moderate — segmenting limits blast radius, data diode prevents pivot to setpoints)

$$R_{residual} = L' \times C' = 1 \times 2 = 2$$

### Step 5 — Classify Residual Risk

$$R_{residual} = 2 \in [1, 4] \Rightarrow \textbf{Low Risk (Acceptable)}$$

### Step 6 — Document and Monitor

Add to the **risk register** with:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Risk ID | RISK-DCS-007 |
| Asset | Engineering Workstation + DCS Controller |
| Threat | Phishing + lateral movement |
| Initial Risk | High (12) |
| Controls | MFA, whitelisting, data diode, training |
| Residual Risk | Low (2) |
| Owner | CISO + Plant Manager |
| Review | Quarterly |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Controls to Reduce Risk

Below is the **control-effectiveness matrix** students can reproduce in exams:

| Control Category | Reduces L (Likelihood) | Reduces C (Consequence) | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Network Segmentation | Yes (high) | Yes (high) | Purdue zones, VLANs, data diodes |
| MFA / Strong Auth | Yes (high) | No | VPN MFA, jump host MFA |
| Patching | Yes (medium) | No | Windows patching, firmware updates |
| Whitelisting | Yes (high) | Yes (medium) | Application allow-lists on HMI |
| Backups (tested) | No | Yes (high) | Offline backups, immutable storage |
| Security Awareness | Yes (medium) | No | Phishing simulations |
| SIEM / Monitoring | Yes (medium — detection) | Yes (medium — fast response) | OT-specific SIEM (e.g., Nozomi, Dragos) |
| Physical Security | Yes (medium) | No | Locked cabinets, CCTV |
| Incident Response Plan | No | Yes (high) | Documented playbooks, tabletop exercises |

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 theory questions often ask: *"Classify three controls that reduce consequence but not likelihood."* Answer: **Backups, IR plans, segmentation** (segmentation does both, so be careful in your answer).

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Risk Identification: IEC 62443-3-2 Zone & Conduit Method

This is a **mandatory KTU topic** for Part B questions. The derivation is:

**Step 1 — Define the System Under Consideration (SUC).**
Example: "The reactor control loop, including PLC, HMI, sensors, and network."

**Step 2 — Partition into Security Zones.**
Group assets that share a common security policy. Example:
- **Zone A:** Reactor PLC + Safety SIS (safety-critical).
- **Zone B:** Engineering Workstation (operator interface).
- **Zone C:** Historian (data repository).

**Step 3 — Identify Conduits.**
Conduits are communication paths *between* zones. Example:
- Conduit 1: Engineering Workstation ↔ PLC (Ethernet/IP).
- Conduit 2: PLC ↔ SIS (safety bus, Modbus).
- Conduit 3: Historian ↔ Enterprise (OPC-UA bridge).

**Step 4 — Assign Security Level Targets (SL-T).**
IEC 62443 defines four Security Level Targets:
- **SL-T 1:** Casual / coincidental violation.
- **SL-T 2:** Intentional violation using simple means / low resources.
- **SL-T 3:** Intentional violation using sophisticated means / moderate resources.
- **SL-T 4:** Intentional violation using sophisticated means / extensive resources (nation-state).

For a reactor PLC, the typical SL-T is **SL-T 3**.

**Step 5 — Apply Foundational Requirements (FR).**
IEC 62443 specifies seven FRs (FR 1: Identification & Authentication, FR 2: Use Control, FR 3: System Integrity, …, FR 7: Resource Availability). Map controls to each FR.

**Step 6 — Calculate the Achieved Security Level (SL-A).**
Compare SL-T vs SL-A. If SL-A < SL-T, the risk is *unacceptable* — apply additional controls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** When asked "How does IEC 62443 differ from NIST SP 800-82?", the answer is: IEC 62443 is **zone-based and security-level driven**; NIST SP 800-82 is **a catalogue of OT-specific recommendations** and is supplementary.

---

## 3.4 Incident Response Lifecycle Derivation (NIST SP 800-61 + ICS Overlay)

The **ICS Incident Response (IR) lifecycle** has four phases; KTU frequently asks for these.

### Phase 1 — Preparation
$$IR_{success} = f\left(People_{trained}, Process_{documented}, Technology_{ready}\right)$$

- Establish an **ICS-specific CSIRT** (Computer Security Incident Response Team).
- Maintain a list of vendor contacts, regulator contacts, and law-enforcement channels.
- Pre-stage forensic tools in a "jump bag" — but **never connect an unverified USB** to a live system.
- Develop **playbooks** for: ransomware, denial-of-service, unauthorized PLC programming change, insider misuse, and physical sabotage.

### Phase 2 — Detection & Analysis
Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) in ICS include:

- Unexpected PLC logic changes.
- Anomalous process variable trends without a corresponding operator action.
- Unauthorized accounts on engineering workstations.
- Unusual Modbus/DNP3 traffic at non-business hours.
- Antivirus alerts (where present).
- SIEM correlation alerts.

### Phase 3 — Containment, Eradication & Recovery
- **Short-term containment:** Isolate the affected zone (block conduit), disable compromised account.
- **Long-term containment:** Apply temporary patch, install clean image.
- **Eradication:** Remove malware, identify root cause (e.g., phishing email).
- **Recovery:** Validate safe state, restore from clean backup, perform **safety integrity check** before resuming operations.

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL ICS Rule:** Never blindly reboot an industrial controller. A reboot may lose logic, lose tuning, or worse — if the controller is currently executing a safety function, a reboot could cause unsafe state. Always **coordinate with operations** before any controller-level action.

### Phase 4 — Post-Incident Activity

- **Lessons-learned report** (within 14 days).
- Update risk register with new threat, vulnerability, and recalculated residual risk.
- Update playbooks.
- Conduct **tabletop exercise** within 90 days to validate the updated response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Purdue Model Reference (KTU 2024):**
> - **Level 0:** Process / Field devices (sensors, actuators).
> - **Level 1:** Basic Control (PLCs, DCS controllers).
> - **Level 2:** Supervisory Control (HMI, SCADA servers).
> - **Level 3:** Operations Management (Historian, MES).
> - **Level 3.5:** IDMZ (Industrial DMZ).
> - **Level 4-5:** Enterprise / Business Network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **IDMZ (Level 3.5)** is the most important architectural control in KTU's risk assessment syllabus. All data flowing between IT and OT must terminate at a **proxy or data diode** in the IDMZ — never allow direct routing.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

> *"Differentiate between IT risk assessment and ICS risk assessment in any three dimensions."*

**Model Answer (Board-Standard):**

| Dimension | IT Risk Assessment | ICS Risk Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Concern** | Confidentiality of data | Safety, availability, and integrity of physical process |
| **System Lifetime** | 3-5 years, frequent refresh | 15-25 years, legacy coexistence is the norm |
| **Patch Cadence** | Days to weeks | Months to years, requires planned shutdown |
| **Downtime Tolerance** | Hours (revenue loss) | Zero seconds (safety and environmental harm) |
| **Threat Model** | Hackers, financial fraud, APTs | Nation-states, insider sabotage, ransomware with physical consequences |
| **Anti-virus Feasibility** | Universal | Often impossible (deterministic controllers) |

**Conclusion:** ICS risk assessment demands a **safety-first, availability-driven** methodology and must align with standards like **IEC 62443-3-2** and **NIST SP 800-82** rather than purely IT frameworks.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

> *"List the four phases of the NIST Incident Response lifecycle and state one ICS-specific consideration in the 'Containment' phase."*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Preparation**
2. **Detection and Analysis**
3. **Containment, Eradication, and Recovery**
4. **Post-Incident Activity**

**ICS-specific consideration during Containment:**
When isolating an affected ICS zone, engineers must **coordinate with operations and safety teams** before disabling any controller, because an unplanned shutdown of a safety-critical system (e.g., Emergency Shutdown System) can itself create a hazardous physical state. Containment should follow the principle of **"isolate, do not power-cycle"** — block the conduit at the network layer, never reboot the controller.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions in this module carry 14 marks with internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below. **Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 3, 14-Mark Variant]**

> *A water treatment plant uses a SCADA system with an engineering workstation, a historian, and a remote vendor access link. The plant has experienced two phishing attempts in the last quarter, and the engineering workstation runs Windows 7 without MFA on the VPN.*
>
> *(a) [7 Marks] Conduct a risk assessment for this scenario using the IEC 62443-3-2 zone-and-conduit approach. Identify assets, threats, vulnerabilities, and inherent risk.*
>
> *(b) [7 Marks] Propose a risk mitigation plan with technical, administrative, and physical controls, and recalculate the residual risk score.*

#### Model Solution for Part A(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — System Under Consideration (SUC):**
The water treatment SCADA environment, including engineering workstation, historian, PLC controlling the chlorination loop, and the remote vendor VPN link.

**Step 2 — Security Zones:**

- **Zone Z1 — Process Control:** Chlorination PLC, sensors, actuators.
- **Zone Z2 — Supervisory:** Engineering workstation, local HMI.
- **Zone Z3 — Data and Enterprise:** Historian, remote vendor link, enterprise network.

**Step 3 — Conduits:**

- **Conduit C1:** Engineering workstation ↔ PLC (Ethernet/IP, Modbus TCP).
- **Conduit C2:** Historian ↔ Engineering workstation (SQL/OPC).
- **Conduit C3:** Remote vendor ↔ Historian (VPN over Internet).

**Step 4 — Critical Assets:**

| Asset | Tier | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Chlorination PLC | Tier 1 (Safety) | Wrong chlorine dose is a public health hazard |
| Engineering Workstation | Tier 2 | Single point of configuration for the plant |
| Historian | Tier 3 | Data integrity, regulatory reporting |
| Vendor VPN | Tier 3 | External attack surface |

**Step 5 — Threats:**

- Phishing email with malware payload.
- Credential theft leading to remote vendor impersonation.
- Ransomware encrypting the engineering workstation.
- Lateral movement from historian to PLC.

**Step 6 — Vulnerabilities:**

- Windows 7 (no longer patched by Microsoft — unpatched CVEs).
- No MFA on VPN.
- Engineering workstation has flat network access to PLC.
- Historian is on the same VLAN as the PLC.
- Vendor link uses persistent VPN credentials.

**Step 7 — Inherent Risk Score (Semi-Quantitative):**

- Likelihood (L) = 4 (Likely — phishing attempts already observed).
- Consequence (C) = 5 (Catastrophic — chlorine mis-dosing can be lethal).

$$R_{inherent} = L \times C = 4 \times 5 = 20 \Rightarrow \textbf{Critical Risk}$$

> Conclusion of Part (a): The system has a *critical* inherent risk; mitigation is mandatory.

#### Model Solution for Part A(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Technical Controls:**

1. **Network Segmentation:** Isolate the PLC in a dedicated VLAN (Purdue Level 1), install a unidirectional gateway between historian and PLC, enforce IDMZ (Level 3.5) between vendor VPN and historian.
2. **Identity & Access:** Enforce **MFA on vendor VPN**, rotate credentials, use just-in-time access tokens.
3. **Endpoint Hardening:** Replace Windows 7 with a supported OS, apply application whitelisting, disable USB storage on engineering workstation.
4. **Patch Management:** Implement a formal OT patch cycle with risk-based prioritisation.
5. **Monitoring:** Deploy an OT-aware IDS/IPS (e.g., Dragos, Nozomi Networks) on Conduit C1 and Conduit C2.
6. **Backups:** Maintain offline, immutable backups of PLC logic and historian database.

**Step 2 — Administrative Controls:**

1. **Security Awareness Training:** Quarterly phishing simulations for all plant staff and vendors.
2. **Phishing-resistant Policy:** Vendor must use FIDO2 tokens; no shared accounts.
3. **Change Management:** All PLC logic changes must be approved by a change advisory board (CAB) and recorded.
4. **Risk Register:** Document this assessment and review every 90 days.

**Step 3 — Physical Controls:**

1. Locked engineering cabinet.
2. Badge-restricted control room.
3. CCTV on plant entry points.
4. Disabling of unused physical ports on the engineering workstation.

**Step 4 — Recalculate Residual Risk:**

- New Likelihood (L') = 2 (Unlikely — MFA + segmentation + whitelisting drastically reduce probability).
- New Consequence (C') = 3 (Moderate — segmentation limits blast radius, but chlorination risk remains).

$$R_{residual} = L' \times C' = 2 \times 3 = 6 \Rightarrow \textbf{Medium Risk}$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**

The residual risk of 6 falls into the *Medium* band (5-9). Per the risk treatment matrix, further reduction is recommended within 6 months. Suggested additional controls: deploy a **safety instrumented function (SIF)** independent of the PLC as a hardware interlock for chlorine dosing, and conduct **annual red-team exercises**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 3, 14-Mark Variant]**

> *(a) [7 Marks] Explain the NIST SP 800-30 risk assessment methodology. List its four components and describe how it differs from a pure qualitative 5×5 matrix approach.*
>
> *(b) [7 Marks] Design an incident response plan for a ransomware attack on a petrochemical plant. Include the four lifecycle phases, key actions, and ICS-specific safeguards.*

#### Model Solution for Part B(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Overview of NIST SP 800-30:**
NIST SP 800-30 Revision 1 provides a comprehensive **risk assessment methodology** for federal information systems and is widely adopted in OT environments with overlays from NIST SP 800-82.

**Step 2 — Four Core Components:**

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| 1. Threat Source Identification | Catalogue adversarial, accidental, structural, and environmental threat sources |
| 2. Vulnerability Identification | Review asset configurations, patch status, and architectural weaknesses |
| 3. Likelihood Determination | Use a combination of qualitative judgment, threat-source capability, and vulnerability severity to assign a likelihood rating |
| 4. Impact / Consequence Determination | Assess the harm to operations, assets, individuals, and the organisation |

These components feed a **risk determination** function.

**Step 3 — Risk Determination Formula (NIST Quantitative):**

$$R = L \times I$$

where $L$ is the likelihood of event occurrence and $I$ is the impact magnitude. NIST extends this with **threat source capability**, **vulnerability severity**, and **existing controls** as likelihood inputs.

**Step 4 — Difference from 5×5 Matrix:**

| Aspect | NIST SP 800-30 | 5×5 Pure Qualitative Matrix |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Hybrid — supports both qualitative and quantitative | Purely qualitative |
| Inputs | Multiple factors (threat capability, vulnerability, controls) | Only L and C as analyst judgement |
| Output | Risk score with detailed justification | Quick categorical risk band |
| Scalability | Suitable for complex multi-asset systems | Suitable for rapid triage |
| Use Case | Formal assessments, regulatory submissions | Initial screening, small plants |

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
NIST SP 800-30 is more rigorous and is preferred for **regulated** and **high-criticality** ICS environments, while the 5×5 matrix is a useful **triage** tool for front-line engineers.

#### Model Solution for Part B(b) [7 Marks]

**Incident Response Plan: Ransomware on a Petrochemical Plant**

**Phase 1 — Preparation (Before the Attack):**

- Establish an **OT-CSIRT** comprising plant operations, safety, IT, and external incident-response retainer.
- Maintain an **offline, immutable backup** of all PLC logic, HMI project files, historian databases, and engineering workstation images.
- **Test backups quarterly** with a documented recovery drill.
- Pre-stage forensic tools in a sealed bag — but **never connect unverified media** to OT systems.
- Conduct **tabletop exercises** every 6 months.
- Maintain a **24×7 incident hotline** with named on-call personnel.

**Phase 2 — Detection and Analysis:**

- OT-aware SIEM detects unusual file encryption patterns on the engineering workstation.
- EDR (where deployed) flags the ransomware family signature.
- Operator notices **PLC HMI displays frozen** and process variables are no longer updating.
- **Triage:** Confirm the scope — is the OT network affected or only the IT side?
- Declare the incident, notify the CSIRT, and start the **incident log**.

**Phase 3 — Containment, Eradication, and Recovery:**

- **Short-term Containment:** Isolate the engineering workstation at the network layer (block conduit). Do **not** power it off (volatile memory evidence).
- **Coordinate with Plant Operations:** Bring the plant to a safe state using the **Emergency Operating Procedure (EOP)** — not by rebooting the controller.
- **Long-term Containment:** Use a clean gold image to restore the workstation; maintain manual operation of the plant in the interim.
- **Eradication:** Identify the phishing entry point, scan all related endpoints, reset all credentials, audit vendor VPN sessions.
- **Recovery:**
  1. Validate the integrity of the **clean backup** of PLC logic.
  2. Compare current logic vs. last known good (CRC check).
  3. Restore the engineering workstation from the verified image.
  4. Conduct a **safety integrity check** with the operations team.
  5. Resume operations under heightened monitoring for 30 days.

**Phase 4 — Post-Incident Activity:**

- **Lessons-Learned Report** within 14 days, including timeline, root cause, control gaps, and financial / safety impact.
- Update the **risk register** — add the new threat (e.g., specific ransomware family), vulnerability (e.g., phishing-prone email gateway), and recalculate residual risk.
- Update **IR playbooks** with the actual attack chain observed.
- Schedule a **post-incident tabletop exercise** within 90 days.
- Consider **regulatory notification** obligations (e.g., NCIIPC in India, CISA in the US, NIS2 in the EU).

**ICS-Specific Safeguards Throughout:**

- **Safety interlock primacy:** Plant safety is never compromised for forensic purposes.
- **No uncontrolled reboots** of controllers.
- **Air-gap verification** for the safety bus (SIS must remain isolated).
- **Manual fallback operations** capability must always be maintained.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Treating Risk as a Single Number:** Many students write "Risk = 12" and stop. Examiners expect you to **interpret the score, map it to a risk band, and justify the treatment choice**.
> 2. **Confusing Likelihood and Impact:** A common error is using *Consequence* values to feed *Likelihood*. L is about probability, C is about severity. Mixing them loses 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring Residual Risk:** The board **explicitly** tests residual risk. If you compute the inherent risk but skip the residual, you lose 3-4 marks on the 14-mark question.
> 4. **IT-Only Examples:** Giving an example like "antivirus on a PLC" or "patch a controller every Tuesday" is a red flag. **Always** call out the OT-specific constraints.
> 5. **Skipping the Asset Tier:** KTU expects a *4-tier* or *safety-critical* classification of assets. Listing assets without prioritisation loses 1-2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting ICS-Specific IR Phrase:** The phrase **"coordinate with operations, do not power-cycle controllers"** is a board favourite. Omit it at your peril.
> 7. **Missing the IDMZ (Purdue Level 3.5):** Any network segmentation answer that omits the IDMZ is incomplete.
> 8. **No Communication Plan:** Risk management is a *socio-technical* discipline. If your answer has no mention of stakeholder communication, you miss 1-2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorise for KTU 2024):**

- **Risk = f(Threat, Vulnerability, Consequence, Likelihood).** A risk exists only when a threat can exploit a vulnerability.
- **Semi-quantitative formula:** $R = L \times C$, with the 5×5 matrix producing 1-25.
- **Inherent Risk → Controls → Residual Risk.** Always recalculate after mitigation.
- **Risk Treatment Options:** Avoid, Reduce, Transfer, Accept (in this order of preference).
- **ICS Priority Order:** Safety > Availability > Integrity > Confidentiality (the **SAIC** mnemonic).
- **IEC 62443-3-2 Zones and Conduits:** Group assets by security policy, not by physical location.
- **Purdue Model:** Levels 0-5, with the **IDMZ at 3.5** as the OT/IT bridge.
- **NIST IR Lifecycle:** Preparation, Detection & Analysis, Containment-Eradication-Recovery, Post-Incident Activity.
- **Critical ICS IR Rule:** Never power-cycle a controller without operations' approval.
- **Asset Tiers:** Safety > Operational Criticality > Business Support > Ancillary.
- **Top OT Threat Actors:** Nation-states, insider sabotage, ransomware (TRITON, INDUSTROYER, BlackEnergy).
- **Top OT Vulnerabilities:** Default creds, no MFA, flat networks, unpatched Windows, insecure remote access.
- **Top OT Controls:** Segmentation, MFA, application whitelisting, OT-aware monitoring, immutable backups, IR playbooks, **safety instrumented systems as last line of defence**.
- **Standards to Know:** IEC 62443-3-2, IEC 62443-3-3, NIST SP 800-82, NIST SP 800-30, NIST CSF, ISO 31000, NCIIPC guidelines.
- **Risk Register Columns:** ID, Asset, Threat, Vulnerability, Inherent Score, Controls, Residual Score, Owner, Review Date.
- **Key Metrics:** RTO, RPO, MTTR, MTBF, Availability (target ≥ 99.99% for safety-critical systems).
- **One-Liner for the Board:** "An ICS risk assessment is not complete until the residual risk is documented, communicated, and reviewed on a defined cadence."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure design and configuration of ICS: best practices for secure system design and architecture, secure configuration of ICS components (e.g., PLCs, HMIs), principles of secure coding and application development for ICS.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT742 - CYBER SECURITY FOR INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Secure design and configuration of ICS: best practices for secure system design and architecture, secure configuration of ICS components (e.g., PLCs, HMIs), principles of secure coding and application development for ICS.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Secure Design and Configuration of ICS (Industrial Control Systems)** refers to the systematic application of engineering principles, architectural patterns, and hardening techniques during the planning, deployment, and operational phases of an ICS, in order to preserve the **confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA Triad)** of the system, its data, and the physical process it controls. According to the **ISA/IEC 62443** standard family, secure design is a *defense-in-depth* lifecycle activity spanning system architecture, component hardening, network segmentation, and secure software development.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The 2024 Scheme emphasizes three pillars:
> 1. **Best practices for secure system design and architecture** (network segmentation, zones & conduits, Purdue Model, defense-in-depth).
> 2. **Secure configuration of ICS components** such as **PLCs** (Programmable Logic Controllers), **HMIs** (Human-Machine Interfaces), **RTUs**, **SCADA servers**, and **Engineering Workstations**.
> 3. **Principles of secure coding and application development** for ICS firmware, ladder logic, HMI scripts, and SCADA applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **industrial plant as a medieval fortress protecting a royal treasury**:

- The **outer wall** = Perimeter network (firewalls, DMZ). The first line of defense.
- The **inner courtyard** = Industrial DMZ separating corporate IT from operational technology (OT).
- The **tower rooms** = Individual ICS zones (Zone 1: Field Devices, Zone 2: Local Control, Zone 3: Site Operations).
- The **guards at each gate** = Authentication, authorization, and access control lists.
- The **locks on the treasure chests** = Hardening configurations on PLCs and HMIs.
- The **scrolls and ledgers** = Log files and audit trails.
- The **master builder's blueprints** = Secure coding standards that prevent structural weaknesses.

If a thief breaks one wall, there must be another wall, another guard, another lock. This layered philosophy is called **Defense-in-Depth** — the cornerstone of ICS secure design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics and Standards Used in ICS Security Design**
> - **IEC 62443-3-3**: System Security Requirements and Security Levels (**SL 1** to **SL 4**).
> - **NIST SP 800-82r3**: Guide to Operational Technology (OT) Security.
> - **ISO/IEC 27001**: Information Security Management.
> - **NERC-CIP**: Critical Infrastructure Protection (for power grids).
> - **IEC 61508 / SIL (Safety Integrity Level)**: Functional safety.
> - **Defense-in-Depth Layers**: **7 layers** (Policy, Physical, Network, Host, Application, Data, Device).

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

Module 4 directly maps to **Course Outcomes (CO)** such as:
- **CO4**: *Design and apply security best practices for industrial automation systems.*

In KTU 2024 End Semester Examinations, students are frequently tested on:
- Mapping attack surfaces to Purdue Model layers.
- Listing hardening steps for PLCs and HMIs.
- Comparing IT vs OT security paradigms.
- Applying defense-in-depth principles in scenario questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Purdue Model / ISA-95 Reference Architecture

The **Purdue Model** is the foundational reference architecture for ICS network segmentation. It divides the industrial enterprise into **six logical levels** grouped into three zones: **Operations (Levels 0–3)**, **Operations Support (Level 3.5 – Industrial DMZ)**, and **Enterprise (Levels 4–5)**.

| Purdue Level | Typical Components | Trust Zone | Security Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 0** | Sensors, Actuators, Motors | Process / Field | Physical security, tamper-proofing |
| **Level 1** | PLCs, RTUs, Basic HMIs | Basic Control | Hardening, firmware integrity |
| **Level 2** | SCADA Servers, Alarms, Historians | Supervisory Control | Access control, patch management |
| **Level 3** | Engineering Workstations, MES | Site Operations | Role-based access, anti-malware |
| **Level 3.5** | Industrial DMZ (IDMZ) | Operations Support | Firewalls, jump hosts, brokers |
| **Level 4** | ERP, Email, File Servers | Enterprise IT | IT-grade controls, email filtering |
| **Level 5** | Internet, Cloud, Vendors | External | VPN, zero-trust remote access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Industrial DMZ (Level 3.5)** is a **buffer zone** that brokers all data flow between IT and OT. No direct TCP/UDP communication should cross between Levels 3 and 4.

## 2.2 Defense-in-Depth: The 7-Layer Model

Defense-in-Depth in ICS combines **physical, network, host, application, data, device, and policy** controls. Each layer is independent — failure of one must not cascade to system compromise.

### Layer 1: Security Policies and Procedures
- Asset inventory and classification.
- Change management and patch policies.
- Incident response playbooks.
- OT-specific cybersecurity training.

### Layer 2: Physical Security
- Locked cabinets for PLCs.
- CCTV monitoring of control rooms.
- Biometric or RFID access to HMI stations.
- Tamper-evident seals on field devices.

### Layer 3: Network Security
- **Network segmentation** using VLANs and zones.
- **Firewalls** between Purdue levels.
- **Unidirectional Gateways / Data Diodes** for high-security zones.
- **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)** specialized for OT protocols (e.g., Suricata with OT rulesets, Dragos, Nozomi Networks).

### Layer 4: Host Security
- Hardening of SCADA servers and engineering workstations.
- Application whitelisting.
- Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) tuned for OT.
- Disabling unused USB ports and services.

### Layer 5: Application Security
- Secure coding of ladder logic, HMI scripts, SCADA applications.
- Input validation in tag addressing.
- Code reviews and static analysis.

### Layer 6: Data Security
- Encryption in transit (TLS, OPC-UA security policies).
- Integrity checks (HMAC, digital signatures for firmware).
- Backups with offline copies.

### Layer 7: Device Security
- Firmware signing and verification.
- Disable unused services on PLCs (e.g., FTP, Telnet, HTTP web servers).
- Logical segmentation within PLCs using user programs and tasks.

## 2.3 Secure Configuration of PLCs

**PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers)** are real-time control devices that must be hardened both **physically** and **logically**.

| Hardening Domain | Best Practice Configuration |
|---|---|
| **Firmware** | Apply signed firmware updates; verify checksums; disable auto-update. |
| **Default Credentials** | Change vendor defaults (e.g., Siemens S7 default password). Enforce strong passwords (≥ **12 characters**, alphanumeric, special). |
| **Network Services** | Disable unused services: FTP, TFTP, Telnet, HTTP, SNMPv1/v2. Enable HTTPS and SNMPv3 with authentication. |
| **Access Control** | Implement role-based access (Operator, Engineer, Maintenance, Auditor). |
| **Communication** | Use encrypted protocols (e.g., S7Comm-Plus with TLS, Modbus over TLS, EtherNet/IP with CIP Security). |
| **Physical Ports** | Lock USB, serial, and Ethernet ports. Disable unused Ethernet ports. |
| **Program Protection** | Enable password protection for PLC program download/upload. Set CPU to "Run/Read-Only" mode where possible. |
| **Audit Logs** | Enable security event logs (logon failures, configuration changes, program downloads). |
| **Watchdog Timers** | Configure fail-safe states for out-of-range conditions. |

### Example: Siemens S7-1500 Hardening Steps (commonly asked in KTU)
1. Set **"Access Protection"** in TIA Portal with strong CPU password.
2. Configure **"Connection Mechanisms"** → Disable PUT/GET communication if unused.
3. Set **"Security Event"** logging to a central SIEM.
4. Enable **"Know-how Protection"** for FB/FC blocks.
5. Disable **Web Server** unless required, and enable HTTPS only.
6. Set **"Fail-Safe Behavior"** to STOP mode on critical errors.

## 2.4 Secure Configuration of HMIs

**HMIs (Human-Machine Interfaces)** are often the weakest link because they run commercial OS (Windows) and face operator screens.

| HMI Hardening Control | Description |
|---|---|
| **OS Hardening** | Use dedicated OS image, disable Windows features, apply CIS Benchmarks. |
| **Account Management** | Separate operator accounts from engineer accounts. No shared logins. |
| **Screen Locking** | Auto-logout after 5–10 minutes of inactivity. |
| **Application Whitelisting** | Only the HMI runtime (e.g., WinCC, iFIX, Ignition) may execute. |
| **Patch Management** | Test patches on staging before HMI deployment. |
| **Backup** | Regular image-based backups stored offline. |
| **Network** | Place HMIs in dedicated VLAN with no internet access. |
| **Input Validation** | Validate operator inputs (range checks, type checks). |

## 2.5 Principles of Secure Coding for ICS Applications

Secure coding in ICS differs from IT due to **real-time constraints, legacy protocols, and safety implications**.

### Key Principles
1. **Input Validation**: Every tag value, sensor reading, and operator input must be validated against expected ranges.
2. **Fail-Safe Defaults**: On invalid input, system should revert to a known safe state.
3. **Least Privilege**: Code modules should have minimum permissions required.
4. **Memory Safety**: Buffer overflow protection in ladder logic and HMI scripting.
5. **Code Signing**: Digitally sign PLC programs and HMI projects to prevent tampering.
6. **Static Analysis**: Use tools (e.g., Codesys Static Analyzer, Rockwell Studio 5000 Logix Designer add-ins) to detect unsafe constructs.
7. **No Hardcoded Credentials**: Secrets must be stored in secure vaults, not in code.
8. **Auditability**: All code changes must be logged, reviewed, and version-controlled.
9. **Separation of Concerns**: Keep safety logic separate from cybersecurity logic.
10. **Test in Staging**: Never test new code directly on production PLCs.

### Common ICS Coding Vulnerabilities (OWASP ICS Top 10 style)
- **CWE-798**: Use of Hard-coded Credentials.
- **CWE-119**: Improper Restriction of Operations within Buffer Bounds.
- **CWE-20**: Improper Input Validation.
- **CWE-306**: Missing Authentication for Critical Function.
- **CWE-862**: Missing Authorization.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although ICS security is largely architectural, certain **risk and security-level computations** are testable.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk** | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Qualitative (Low/Med/High) or numeric score |
| **Defense-in-Depth Coverage** | $C_{\text{DiD}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{7} w_i \cdot L_i}{7}$ where $w_i$ is weight, $L_i \in \{0,1\}$ implementation | Score between 0 and 1 |
| **Security Level (SL)** | $\text{SL-T} = \text{SL-C} + \Delta_{\text{com}}$ where $\Delta_{\text{com}}$ is communication adjustment | SL 1, SL 2, SL 3, SL 4 |
| **Mean Time to Patch (MTTP)** | $\text{MTTP} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{\text{deployed},i} - t_{\text{released},i})$ | Days |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
| **Purdue Layer Trust** | Decreasing trust from Level 5 (lowest) to Level 0 (highest) | Logical zones |
| **Zone-Conduit Model** | $\text{Security} = f(\text{Zone isolation}, \text{Conduit controls})$ | IEC 62443 |

> [!NOTE]
> Use **\vert** or **\mid** notation for absolute value to avoid markdown table breakage: e.g., write $\vert x \vert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ inside table cells.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants**: Implement Purdue + IDMZ to prevent grid-takover attacks (e.g., Ukraine 2015 BlackEnergy).
- **Water Treatment**: PLC hardening prevents chemical dosing manipulation.
- **Manufacturing**: HMI lockdown prevents ransomware propagation (e.g., Honda 2017 WannaCry).
- **Oil & Gas**: Unidirectional gateways protect upstream SCADA from ransomware.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Design Methodology

## 3.1 Systematic Step-by-Step ICS Secure Design Methodology

The **IEC 62443-3-3** secure design process follows a rigorous, repeatable methodology. Below is the exhaustive 8-step process KTU examiners expect students to describe in 14-mark answers.

### Step 1: Asset Identification and System Under Consideration (SuC) Definition

**Goal**: Enumerate all assets (physical, logical, software, data) and their interconnections.

**Activities**:
- Build an **asset inventory** including PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, network switches, routers, firewalls, servers, engineering workstations.
- Capture metadata: model, firmware version, IP/MAC, location, owner, criticality rating.
- Map **data flows** using tools like **Wireshark captures**, **network diagrams**, or **PAS Cyber Integrity**.

**Output**: System Description Document and Asset Inventory Database.

### Step 2: Zoning and Conduit Definition

**Goal**: Group assets into **zones** based on functional and security requirements; define **conduits** (logical communication paths) between zones.

**Process**:
1. Identify assets that share similar security requirements.
2. Cluster them into zones (e.g., Boiler Control Zone, Cooling Tower Zone, Operator HMI Zone).
3. Identify all communication paths → each path becomes a conduit.
4. Assign each conduit a **Security Level Target (SL-T)** based on worst-case impact.

$$\text{SL-T}_{\text{conduit}} = \max_{a \in \text{conduit}} \text{Impact}(a)$$

### Step 3: Security Level Target (SL-T) Selection

For each zone, select an SL-T from **SL 1** (casual) to **SL 4** (state-sponsored adversary).

| SL | Adversary | Example Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **SL 1** | Casual / opportunistic | Office IT systems |
| **SL 2** | Intentional with simple means | General manufacturing |
| **SL 3** | Sophisticated adversary | Critical infrastructure |
| **SL 4** | State-sponsored | Power grid, nuclear |

### Step 4: Security Requirements Derivation (SR)

Map each SL-T to the **System Security Requirements (SR)** from IEC 62443-3-3. There are **7 Foundational Requirements (FR)**:

- **FR 1**: Identification and Authentication Control.
- **FR 2**: Use Control.
- **FR 3**: System Integrity.
- **FR 4**: Data Confidentiality.
- **FR 5**: Restricted Data Flow.
- **FR 6**: Timely Response to Events.
- **FR 7**: Resource Availability.

Each FR has multiple **System Requirements (SR)** with increasing rigor at higher SLs.

### Step 5: Security Measures Selection

Select technical, procedural, and physical countermeasures per zone and conduit.

| Layer | Countermeasure Example |
|---|---|
| Network | VLAN, ACL, firewall, data diode |
| Host | Patch, AV, application whitelisting |
| Application | Input validation, code signing |
| Physical | Locks, CCTV, mantraps |
| Policy | Change management, training |

### Step 6: Risk Assessment and Residual Risk

For each threat, calculate the risk:

$$\text{Risk} = T \times V \times I$$

where $T$ = Threat likelihood, $V$ = Vulnerability, $I$ = Impact.

**Residual risk** after applying controls:

$$\text{Risk}_{\text{residual}} = \text{Risk}_{\text{original}} - \text{Mitigation Effectiveness}$$

If $\text{Risk}_{\text{residual}} > \text{Risk}_{\text{acceptable}}$, additional controls must be applied.

### Step 7: Implementation and Verification

Deploy controls in this order:
1. **Network segmentation** first (largest attack-surface reduction).
2. **Host hardening** of servers and workstations.
3. **Device hardening** of PLCs, RTUs, HMIs.
4. **Application controls** (whitelisting, secure coding).
5. **Monitoring** (SIEM, IDS, log collection).

**Verification methods**:
- Vulnerability scanning (e.g., Tenable.ot, Qualys OT).
- Penetration testing (in staging).
- Configuration audits against CIS Benchmarks.
- Tabletop exercises.

### Step 8: Continuous Monitoring and Improvement

ICS security is **never finished**. Establish:
- 24/7 SOC monitoring with OT-trained analysts.
- Regular re-assessments (quarterly or after any major change).
- Patch cycles aligned with vendor release schedules.
- Threat intelligence feeds (Dragos, Claroty, ICS-CERT advisories).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: IT vs OT Security Design

| Parameter | IT (Information Technology) | OT (Operational Technology) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA) | **Availability, Integrity, Confidentiality (AIC)** |
| **Patch Cycle** | Days to weeks | Months to years (downtime cost) |
| **Lifetime** | 3–5 years | **15–30 years** |
| **OS** | Modern, patchable | Legacy (Windows XP, VxWorks, custom RTOS) |
| **Network** | Standard TCP/IP | Mixed (serial, fieldbus, industrial Ethernet) |
| **Risk Impact** | Data breach | **Physical safety, environmental, economic** |
| **Default State** | Patch and update | **Do not touch** (working system) |
| **Antivirus** | Standard EDR | Often **not feasible** (real-time impact) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of OT Security**: *Do no harm to the process.* Any security control that risks process disruption must be carefully validated.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Hardening a Hypothetical PLC (Detailed Walkthrough)

**Scenario**: A KTU examiner asks you to harden a Siemens S7-1200 PLC for a water-treatment plant.

**Step 1**: Document the device — model, firmware, location, network segment.
**Step 2**: Physically secure the PLC inside a locked cabinet.
**Step 3**: Change the default password; enforce a 12-character complex password.
**Step 4**: Disable the **Web Server** (unauthenticated by default).
**Step 5**: Disable **PUT/GET** communication unless explicitly required.
**Step 6**: Enable **"Access Protection"** for the CPU.
**Step 7**: Apply **Know-How Protection** to all FB and FC blocks.
**Step 8**: Configure **"Security Events"** to be sent via Syslog to a central SIEM.
**Step 9**: Place the PLC in a **dedicated VLAN** with no direct internet routing.
**Step 10**: Enable **SNMPv3** with authentication if monitoring is needed.
**Step 11**: Validate all changes in **staging environment** before production.
**Step 12**: Document the configuration in a **Hardening Baseline Document**.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Secure HMI Development Lifecycle

1. **Requirement Gathering**: Capture operator workflow, alarm handling, role separation.
2. **Threat Modeling**: Identify misuse cases (e.g., unauthorized parameter changes).
3. **Secure Design**: Use role-based access, screen-level permissions.
4. **Coding**: Apply input validation; avoid hardcoded credentials; use signed scripts.
5. **Testing**: Conduct static analysis, dynamic testing in staging.
6. **Deployment**: Whitelist runtime, lock down OS, restrict USB.
7. **Maintenance**: Patch OS, log events, conduct periodic audits.

## 3.5 Detailed Algorithm: Zone-Conduit Security Level Computation

**Input**: Set of zones $Z = \{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_n\}$ and conduits $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_m\}$.

**Process**:
1. For each zone $z_i$, assign a base Security Level Target $\text{SL-T}(z_i) \in \{1,2,3,4\}$.
2. For each conduit $c_k$ connecting $z_i$ and $z_j$, compute:
$$\text{SL-T}(c_k) = \max(\text{SL-T}(z_i), \text{SL-T}(z_j))$$
3. For each conduit, verify that the required **System Requirements (SR)** are met:
   - If **all** SRs satisfied → **Status: Achieved (SL-A)**.
   - If **partially** satisfied → **Status: Partial (SL-P)** with gap analysis.
4. Document the gap: $\text{Gap} = \text{SL-T} - \text{SL-A}$.
5. Apply compensating controls to close the gap or formally accept residual risk.

**Output**: Zone-Conduit Security Level Achievement Matrix.

$$\text{SL-A}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{k=1}^{m} \text{SL-A}(c_k)$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Purdue Model with Secure Zones (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    L5["Level 5 Enterprise Internet"] --> L4["Level 4 Enterprise IT Zone"]
    L4 --> IDMZ["Level 3.5 Industrial DMZ with Firewalls"]
    IDMZ --> L3["Level 3 Site Operations MES EWS"]
    L3 --> L2["Level 2 Supervisory SCADA Historian"]
    L2 --> L1["Level 1 Basic Control PLCs RTUs"]
    L1 --> L0["Level 0 Field Sensors Actuators"]
    
    IDMZ --> FW1["Perimeter Firewall"]
    FW1 --> IDMZ
    IDMZ --> FW2["Internal Firewall"]
    FW2 --> L3
    
    L3 --> JH["Jump Host for Vendor Access"]
    L2 --> DIAG["Unidirectional Gateway to Historian"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `L5`, `IDMZ`, `FW1`). Labels use plain text without markdown formatting to ensure clean Mermaid rendering.

## 4.2 Defense-in-Depth Layered Architecture (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1["Layer 1 Security Policies and Governance"] --> L2["Layer 2 Physical Security Locks CCTV"]
    L2 --> L3["Layer 3 Network Security Firewalls VLAN IDS"]
    L3 --> L4["Layer 4 Host Security Hardening Whitelisting EDR"]
    L4 --> L5["Layer 5 Application Security Secure Coding Input Validation"]
    L5 --> L6["Layer 6 Data Security Encryption Integrity Backups"]
    L6 --> L7["Layer 7 Device Security Firmware Signing Port Lockdown"]
```

## 4.3 Secure Configuration Workflow for PLCs (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["Step 1 Asset Inventory"] --> A2["Step 2 Baseline Config"]
    A2 --> A3["Step 3 Disable Default Services"]
    A3 --> A4["Step 4 Change Default Credentials"]
    A4 --> A5["Step 5 Apply Network Segmentation VLAN"]
```
